_meta.description: Comprehensive medical knowledge database. 186 diagnoses across 16 categories.

Sepsis.pe_findings: Flushed skin, tachycardia, rapid capillary refill, possible altered mental status

Antibiotics.rationale: Each hour of delay increases mortality 7.6%

Fluid Resuscitation.rationale: 30 mL/kg crystalloid within 3 hours per Surviving Sepsis Campaign

Blood Cultures.rationale: Must be drawn before first antibiotic dose

Myocardial Infarction.pe_findings: Diaphoresis, pale, anxious, possible JVD or lung crackles

12-Lead ECG.rationale: AHA mandates ECG within 10 minutes of arrival

PCI Activation.rationale: Door-to-balloon time <90 min per ACC/AHA guidelines

IM Epinephrine.rationale: First-line treatment; delay significantly increases risk of fatal outcome

IV Fluid Bolus.rationale: Up to 35% of circulating volume can shift out of vasculature

Airway Assessment.rationale: Angioedema can progress to complete obstruction within minutes

Pulmonary Embolism.pe_findings: Tachycardia, tachypnea, possible decreased breath sounds

Anticoagulation.rationale: Heparin or LMWH initiated as soon as PE is suspected with high probability

CTPA or V/Q Scan.rationale: Definitive imaging needed to confirm diagnosis

Thrombolysis Assessment.rationale: For massive PE with hemodynamic instability, consider tPA

DKA.pe_findings: Fruity breath odor, Kussmaul respirations, dehydration signs

Insulin Drip.rationale: 0.1 units/kg/hr after initial fluid resuscitation and potassium check

Fluid Resuscitation.rationale: Average 6-9L deficit; 1-1.5L NS in first hour

Potassium Check.rationale: Must verify K >3.3 before insulin; hypokalemia is lethal

Asthma Exacerbation.pe_findings: Wheezing, reduced air movement, accessory muscle use

Systemic Corticosteroids.rationale: Reduces inflammation; takes 4-6h to peak effect

Ipratropium Bromide.rationale: Additive bronchodilation with albuterol in severe exacerbation

Stroke.pe_findings: Facial droop, unilateral weakness, speech difficulty, possible gaze deviation

CT Head.rationale: Must differentiate ischemic from hemorrhagic before any thrombolytics

tPA Administration.rationale: 4.5-hour window from symptom onset; door-to-needle <60 min

NIHSS Assessment.rationale: Quantifies deficit severity, guides treatment decisions

Pneumonia.pe_findings: Crackles/rhonchi on auscultation, productive cough, febrile, tachypneic

Blood Cultures.rationale: Draw before antibiotics if possible; required for CAP with sepsis

Antibiotics.rationale: Within 4 hours of presentation per CMS/Joint Commission

Chest X-Ray.rationale: Confirm infiltrate and rule out complications

GI Bleed.pe_findings: Pale, diaphoretic, melena or hematemesis, orthostatic hypotension, tachycardia

Type and Crossmatch.rationale: Must be ready for transfusion; GI bleeds are unpredictable

GI Consult.rationale: Endoscopy within 24h for most; emergent if hemodynamically unstable

Transfusion Threshold.rationale: Transfuse if Hgb <7 (or <9 with cardiac disease)

CHF Exacerbation.pe_findings: JVD, bilateral crackles, peripheral edema, S3 gallop, orthopnea

IV Diuresis.rationale: Furosemide to reduce volume overload; adjust for renal function

Non-Invasive Ventilation.rationale: BiPAP/CPAP reduces intubation rate in acute pulmonary edema

Vasodilator Assessment.rationale: Nitroglycerin if SBP >110; reduces preload and afterload

Overdose.pe_findings: Pinpoint pupils (opioid), decreased responsiveness, shallow respirations, cool skin

Naloxone.rationale: 0.4-2mg IV/IM for suspected opioid; titrate to respiratory effort

Airway Protection.rationale: GCS <8 or loss of gag reflex requires intubation

Toxicology Screen.rationale: Identifies coingestants; guides specific antidote therapy

Seizure.pe_findings: Post-ictal confusion, tongue laceration, incontinence, tachycardia, diaphoresis

Benzodiazepine.rationale: Lorazepam 4mg IV or midazolam 10mg IM; status epilepticus after 5 min

Glucose Check.rationale: Hypoglycemia is a reversible cause; dextrose if <60

Second-Line AED.rationale: Levetiracetam or fosphenytoin if seizure persists after 2 doses of benzo

Hypoglycemia.pe_findings: Diaphoresis, tremor, confusion or agitation, tachycardia

Point-of-Care Glucose.rationale: Immediate bedside glucose check; do not wait for lab

Dextrose or Glucagon.rationale: D50 25g IV (or glucagon 1mg IM if no IV access); recheck in 15 min

Identify Cause.rationale: Insulin excess, sulfonylurea, sepsis, adrenal insufficiency, liver failure

Pneumothorax.pe_findings: Absent breath sounds unilaterally, tracheal deviation, JVD, hyperresonance to percussion

Needle Decompression.rationale: Immediate 14-16g needle at 2nd ICS MCL for tension pneumothorax

Portable CXR.rationale: Confirm diagnosis if hemodynamically stable; do NOT delay decompression if tension

Meningitis.pe_findings: Nuchal rigidity, photophobia, petechial rash (if meningococcal), Kernig/Brudzinski positive

Empiric Antibiotics.rationale: Ceftriaxone + vancomycin; do NOT delay for LP if clinically suspected

Dexamethasone.rationale: Give before or with first antibiotic dose; reduces mortality in pneumococcal

Lumbar Puncture.rationale: After CT if indicated; CSF culture is gold standard but never delay abx

Hyperkalemia.pe_findings: Bradycardia, muscle weakness, peaked T-waves on ECG, possible cardiac arrhythmia

12-Lead ECG.rationale: Assess for peaked T-waves, widened QRS, sine wave — dictates urgency

IV Calcium Gluconate.rationale: Cardiac membrane stabilization if ECG changes present; does not lower K

Insulin + Glucose.rationale: 10 units regular insulin + D50; shifts K intracellularly within 20 min

Ectopic Pregnancy.pe_findings: Lower abdominal tenderness, guarding, vaginal bleeding, signs of hypovolemic shock

Quantitative hCG.rationale: Confirms pregnancy; level guides imaging interpretation

Transvaginal Ultrasound.rationale: No IUP with positive hCG = ectopic until proven otherwise

OB/GYN Consult.rationale: Surgical intervention may be emergent if ruptured

Thyroid Storm.pe_findings: Severe tachycardia, hyperthermia, agitation/delirium, tremor, lid lag, diaphoresis

Beta Blocker.rationale: Propranolol or esmolol to control HR; also inhibits T4→T3 conversion

Stress-Dose Steroids.rationale: Hydrocortisone 100mg IV; prevents adrenal crisis and inhibits T4→T3

CPR Initiation.rationale: High-quality CPR within 60 seconds; compressions 100-120/min, 2-inch depth

Defibrillation.rationale: If shockable rhythm (VF/pVT); every minute of delay reduces survival 7-10%

Epinephrine.rationale: 1mg IV/IO every 3-5 min; earlier dosing in non-shockable rhythms per AHA 2020

Aortic Dissection.pe_findings: Severe tearing chest/back pain, blood pressure differential between arms, wide mediastinum on CXR, diaphoresis

Blood Pressure Control.rationale: Target SBP 100-120 to prevent propagation; IV esmolol or nitroprusside

CT Angiography.rationale: Emergent imaging to classify Stanford type A vs B

Surgical Consult.rationale: Type A requires emergent surgical repair

Cardiac Tamponade.pe_findings: Beck triad: hypotension, JVD, muffled heart sounds; pulsus paradoxus >10 mmHg; tachycardia

Bedside Echo.rationale: POCUS to confirm pericardial effusion and RV collapse

Atrial Fibrillation with RVR.pe_findings: Irregularly irregular rhythm, tachycardia, possible dyspnea, palpitations, mild diaphoresis

Rate Control.rationale: IV diltiazem or metoprolol to target HR <110

12-Lead ECG.rationale: Confirm AFib, rule out STEMI or WPW

SVT.pe_findings: Regular narrow-complex tachycardia, palpitations, lightheadedness, anxiety, mild diaphoresis

Vagal Maneuvers.rationale: First-line: modified Valsalva or carotid massage

Adenosine.rationale: 6mg rapid IV push if vagal maneuvers fail; may repeat 12mg

Hypertensive Emergency.pe_findings: Severe headache, visual changes, chest pain, possible papilledema, AMS, epistaxis

IV Antihypertensive.rationale: Nicardipine or clevidipine drip; reduce MAP by 20-25% in first hour

Target Organ Assessment.rationale: CT head, ECG, renal function — identify end-organ damage

Third-Degree Heart Block.pe_findings: Bradycardia, syncope or near-syncope, cannon A waves on JVP, hypotension, fatigue

Transcutaneous Pacing.rationale: Immediate if symptomatic; capture at lowest mA

Atropine.rationale: 0.5mg IV q3-5min; may be ineffective in infranodal block

Transvenous Pacing.rationale: Definitive temporary pacing if TCP ineffective

Cardiogenic Shock.pe_findings: Cool, clammy extremities, JVD, pulmonary crackles, S3 gallop, altered mental status, oliguria

Vasopressor Initiation.rationale: Norepinephrine first-line per SOAP II trial

Bedside Echo.rationale: Assess EF, wall motion, valvular pathology

Mechanical Support Eval.rationale: Consider IABP, Impella, or ECMO if refractory

Unstable Angina.pe_findings: Substernal chest pressure, diaphoresis, radiating to left arm/jaw, mild dyspnea, anxious

12-Lead ECG.rationale: Look for ST depression, T-wave inversions

Aspirin + Heparin.rationale: Dual antiplatelet + anticoagulation per ACS protocol

Nitroglycerin.rationale: SL NTG for symptom relief; avoid if RV infarct or PDE5 inhibitor use

COPD Exacerbation.pe_findings: Barrel chest, pursed-lip breathing, prolonged expiratory phase, diffuse wheezes and rhonchi, accessory muscle use, tripod positioning

BiPAP.rationale: Non-invasive ventilation if persistent hypercapnia; avoid over-oxygenation

Epiglottitis.pe_findings: Tripod positioning, drooling, muffled hot-potato voice, stridor, sore throat, toxic appearance, trismus

Airway Assessment.rationale: Do NOT examine pharynx unless prepared for emergent airway

ENT/Anesthesia Consult.rationale: Double setup: OR for awake fiberoptic + surgical airway backup

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Ceftriaxone + steroids (dexamethasone)

Croup.pe_findings: Barking seal-like cough, inspiratory stridor, hoarse voice, steeple sign on AP neck X-ray, intercostal retractions

Nebulized Epinephrine.rationale: Racemic epinephrine for moderate-severe croup; observe 2-4h for rebound

Dexamethasone.rationale: 0.6 mg/kg PO/IM single dose; effective within 1 hour

Airway Monitoring.rationale: Continuous SpO2 and work of breathing assessment

ARDS.pe_findings: Severe dyspnea, diffuse bilateral crackles, cyanosis, accessory muscle use, diaphoresis, bilateral infiltrates on CXR

Intubation.rationale: Low tidal volume ventilation 6 mL/kg IBW per ARDSNet protocol

PEEP Optimization.rationale: Titrate PEEP to optimize recruitment; target PaO2/FiO2 >150

Prone Positioning.rationale: For P/F ratio <150; 16+ hours prone per PROSEVA trial

Foreign Body Aspiration.pe_findings: Acute onset choking, stridor or wheezing, unilateral decreased breath sounds, cyanosis, agitation, inability to speak if complete obstruction

BLS Airway Maneuvers.rationale: Back blows/abdominal thrusts for complete obstruction

Direct Laryngoscopy.rationale: Attempt visualization and removal with Magill forceps

Smoke Inhalation.pe_findings: Carbonaceous sputum, singed nasal hairs, hoarse voice, stridor, facial burns, soot in oropharynx, tachypnea

100% O2 via NRB.rationale: High-flow O2 immediately; SpO2 unreliable with CO poisoning

Early Intubation.rationale: Intubate before airway edema progresses; direct visualization

Cyanide Antidote.rationale: Hydroxocobalamin if altered mental status with lactic acidosis

Status Epilepticus.pe_findings: Continuous or recurrent seizures >5 min, tonic-clonic activity, cyanosis, incontinence, foaming at mouth, tongue laceration

Benzodiazepine.rationale: Lorazepam 0.1mg/kg IV (max 4mg) or midazolam 10mg IM; repeat once if needed

Second-line AED.rationale: Levetiracetam 60mg/kg, fosphenytoin 20mg PE/kg, or valproate 40mg/kg IV

Glucose Check.rationale: Immediate POC glucose; D50 if hypoglycemic

Subarachnoid Hemorrhage.pe_findings: Thunderclap headache (worst of life), nuchal rigidity, photophobia, possible decreased consciousness, focal neuro deficits, nausea/vomiting

CT Head.rationale: Non-contrast CT; sensitivity >95% within 6 hours of onset

BP Control.rationale: Target SBP <160 until aneurysm secured; nicardipine or labetalol

Neurosurgery Consult.rationale: Emergent for aneurysm clipping or endovascular coiling

Lumbar Puncture.rationale: If CT negative but clinical suspicion high; look for xanthochromia

Spinal Cord Injury.pe_findings: Neurogenic shock: bradycardia + hypotension + warm extremities; sensory level deficit, flaccid paralysis below level, priapism, loss of rectal tone

MAP Target.rationale: Maintain MAP >85 for 7 days per AANS guidelines; vasopressors PRN

MRI Spine.rationale: Urgent imaging to define level and extent of injury

Guillain-Barre Syndrome.pe_findings: Ascending symmetric weakness, areflexia, paresthesias, possible facial diplegia, difficulty swallowing, autonomic instability (BP lability, tachycardia)

NIF/VC Monitoring.rationale: Negative inspiratory force and vital capacity q2-4h; intubate if NIF <-20 or VC <20mL/kg

IVIG or Plasmapheresis.rationale: Start within first 2 weeks of symptom onset; equally effective

DVT Prophylaxis.rationale: Immobilized patients at high risk; enoxaparin or SCDs

Increased ICP.pe_findings: Cushing triad: hypertension + bradycardia + irregular respirations; altered consciousness, unilateral dilated pupil, papilledema, vomiting

HOB Elevation.rationale: Elevate head of bed 30 degrees; midline head position

Mannitol/Hypertonic Saline.rationale: Mannitol 1g/kg or 23.4% saline 30mL for acute herniation

CT Head.rationale: Emergent non-contrast CT to identify cause

Neurosurgery Consult.rationale: For EVD placement or surgical decompression

Epidural Hematoma.pe_findings: Lucid interval followed by rapid decline, unilateral dilated pupil (ipsilateral), contralateral hemiparesis, temporal skull fracture, Cushing response

Mannitol.rationale: Temporizing if signs of herniation while awaiting OR

Acute Pancreatitis.pe_findings: Epigastric pain radiating to back, tenderness with guarding, distended abdomen, tachycardia, possible Cullen or Grey Turner signs if hemorrhagic

Aggressive IV Fluids.rationale: Lactated Ringers 200-500mL/hr initially per ACG guidelines

Pain Management.rationale: IV hydromorphone or morphine; NSAIDs adjunct

NPO.rationale: Nothing by mouth until pain improving; advance diet cautiously

Bowel Obstruction.pe_findings: Distended abdomen, high-pitched tinkling bowel sounds (early) or absent (late), diffuse tenderness, vomiting (bilious or feculent), inability to pass flatus

NGT Decompression.rationale: NG tube to low intermittent suction

CT Abdomen/Pelvis.rationale: IV contrast to evaluate transition point and identify strangulation

Surgery Consult.rationale: Emergent if signs of strangulation, perforation, or closed-loop

Appendicitis.pe_findings: RLQ tenderness at McBurney point, positive Rovsing sign, rebound tenderness, guarding, anorexia, nausea, migration of pain from periumbilical to RLQ

Surgery Consult.rationale: Appendectomy within 24 hours; emergent if perforation suspected

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Cefoxitin or pip-tazo if perforated; pre-op coverage

Cholecystitis.pe_findings: RUQ tenderness, positive Murphy sign, guarding, fever, nausea/vomiting, pain radiating to right scapula

RUQ Ultrasound.rationale: Gallstones, wall thickening >4mm, pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Cefoxitin or pip-tazo for infectious cholecystitis

Surgery Consult.rationale: Cholecystectomy within 72 hours per Tokyo Guidelines

Hepatic Encephalopathy.pe_findings: Altered mental status, asterixis (flapping tremor), fetor hepaticus, jaundice, ascites, spider angiomata, palmar erythema, confusion

Lactulose.rationale: 30mL PO/PR q1-2h until 3-4 BMs/day; first-line treatment

Infection Workup.rationale: Precipitant search: SBP paracentesis, UA, CXR, blood cultures

Mesenteric Ischemia.pe_findings: Severe abdominal pain out of proportion to exam, abdominal distension, bloody stool (late), peritoneal signs (late), tachycardia

CT Angiography.rationale: Mesenteric CTA is gold standard for diagnosis

Vascular Surgery Consult.rationale: Emergent for embolectomy, bypass, or bowel resection

Broad-Spectrum Antibiotics.rationale: Gut flora coverage: pip-tazo or meropenem

Anticoagulation.rationale: Heparin drip for arterial embolism per AGA guidelines

Acute Kidney Injury.pe_findings: Decreased urine output, peripheral edema, JVD if fluid overloaded, possible uremic frost, asterixis, nausea

ECG.rationale: Check for hyperkalemia changes (peaked T, wide QRS)

Nephrology Consult.rationale: For dialysis if refractory hyperkalemia, acidosis, fluid overload, or uremia

Foley Catheter.rationale: Rule out post-renal obstruction; monitor UOP

Hyponatremia.pe_findings: Confusion, headache, nausea/vomiting, lethargy, seizures if severe (<120), cerebral edema signs, muscle weakness

Sodium Level.rationale: Confirm with repeat BMP; assess volume status and chronicity

Hypertonic Saline.rationale: 3% NaCl 100mL bolus for severe symptoms (seizures, coma); max correction 8mEq/24h

Rhabdomyolysis.pe_findings: Muscle pain and weakness, dark tea-colored urine, swollen tender muscles, possible compartment syndrome, tachycardia

Aggressive IV Fluids.rationale: NS 200-300mL/hr; target UOP 200-300mL/hr to prevent ATN

Electrolyte Correction.rationale: Calcium gluconate if hyperkalemic; avoid calcium otherwise (risk of deposition)

Compartment Pressure.rationale: Check if clinical concern; fasciotomy if >30mmHg

Tumor Lysis Syndrome.pe_findings: Nausea, vomiting, muscle cramps, tetany, seizures, arrhythmia, oliguria, lethargy

Rasburicase.rationale: 0.2mg/kg IV for severe hyperuricemia; contraindicated in G6PD deficiency

Nephrology Consult.rationale: Emergent dialysis if refractory hyperkalemia or oliguric renal failure

Adrenal Crisis.pe_findings: Refractory hypotension despite fluids, abdominal pain, nausea/vomiting, confusion, hyperpigmentation (if chronic), weakness, dehydration

IV Hydrocortisone.rationale: 100mg IV push STAT; do not wait for cortisol results if clinically suspected

Aggressive IV Fluids.rationale: NS or D5NS 1-2L bolus; these patients are severely volume depleted

Dextrose.rationale: D50 if hypoglycemic; D5 maintenance after

HHS.pe_findings: Profound dehydration, altered mental status, tachycardia, hypotension, warm dry skin, no Kussmaul respirations (unlike DKA), possible focal neuro deficits

Aggressive IV Fluids.rationale: NS 1-1.5L/hr first hour; switch to 0.45% if corrected Na normal/elevated

Insulin Drip.rationale: Start AFTER adequate volume resuscitation; 0.1-0.14 units/kg/hr

Electrolyte Monitoring.rationale: K+ q1-2h; replace aggressively to maintain >3.5

Myxedema Coma.pe_findings: Hypothermia, bradycardia, altered mental status/coma, non-pitting edema (myxedema), macroglossia, delayed DTRs, hypoventilation

IV Hydrocortisone.rationale: 100mg IV before T4 — rule out concurrent adrenal insufficiency

Passive Rewarming.rationale: Warm blankets; active rewarming can cause cardiovascular collapse

Intubation.rationale: If GCS depressed or hypoventilation; these patients desaturate rapidly

Hemorrhagic Shock.pe_findings: Pale, cool, diaphoretic, tachycardic, hypotensive, altered mental status, weak thready pulse, delayed cap refill >3s, visible hemorrhage or distended abdomen

Hemorrhage Control.rationale: Direct pressure, tourniquet, or surgical intervention

Permissive Hypotension.rationale: Target SBP 80-90 until surgical control; avoid crystalloid overload

TXA.rationale: 1g IV within 3 hours of injury per CRASH-2 trial; then 1g over 8h

Traumatic Brain Injury.pe_findings: Altered consciousness, pupil asymmetry, GCS depression, scalp laceration/hematoma, Battle sign or raccoon eyes, CSF rhinorrhea/otorrhea, focal deficits

CT Head.rationale: Non-contrast CT STAT; identify bleeds, midline shift, fractures

ICP Management.rationale: HOB 30°, midline position, avoid hypotension (SBP >100) and hypoxia (SpO2 >92%)

Neurosurgery Consult.rationale: For surgical lesions: EDH, SDH with shift, depressed fractures

Seizure Prophylaxis.rationale: Levetiracetam 1g IV for 7 days per BTF guidelines

Major Burns.pe_findings: Burn wound assessment (partial vs full thickness), pain, edema, erythema or white/charred tissue, circumferential burn concern, singed hair if inhalation

Parkland Formula Fluids.rationale: 4mL x kg x %TBSA; give half in first 8h from time of burn

Airway Assessment.rationale: Early intubation if inhalation injury suspected — do not wait for edema

Escharotomy.rationale: For circumferential full-thickness burns compromising perfusion or ventilation

Tetanus Prophylaxis.rationale: Tdap if not current; TIG if unknown history

Pelvic Fracture.pe_findings: Pelvic instability on compression, perineal ecchymosis, leg length discrepancy, blood at urethral meatus, hypotension despite fluids

Pelvic Binder.rationale: Circumferential compression at level of greater trochanters

Massive Transfusion Protocol.rationale: Activate early; these fractures bleed significantly

CT Angiography.rationale: Identify arterial bleeding for IR embolization

Retrograde Urethrogram.rationale: Before Foley if blood at urethral meatus

Eclampsia.pe_findings: Tonic-clonic seizures in pregnancy (>20wk), severe hypertension, headache, visual changes, RUQ pain, hyperreflexia with clonus, facial/peripheral edema

Magnesium Sulfate.rationale: 4-6g IV loading over 20min then 1-2g/hr; first-line for seizure prevention and treatment

IV Antihypertensive.rationale: IV labetalol 20mg or hydralazine 5mg; target SBP <160, DBP <110

Delivery Planning.rationale: Definitive treatment is delivery; consult OB stat

Placental Abruption.pe_findings: Vaginal bleeding (may be concealed), rigid board-like uterus, severe abdominal pain, fetal distress, signs of hypovolemic shock, DIC if severe

Type and Crossmatch.rationale: Order 4 units pRBCs immediately; activate MTP if severe

Fetal Monitoring.rationale: Continuous CTG; persistent late decels or bradycardia = emergent delivery

OB Consult.rationale: Emergent cesarean delivery if hemodynamically unstable or fetal distress

Postpartum Hemorrhage.pe_findings: Excessive vaginal bleeding post-delivery, boggy uterus (if atony), tachycardia, hypotension, pallor, diaphoresis, altered mental status if severe

Uterine Massage.rationale: Bimanual fundal massage for uterine atony — most common cause

Uterotonics.rationale: Oxytocin 40U in 1L NS, methylergonovine, carboprost, misoprostol

TXA.rationale: 1g IV within 3 hours of delivery per WOMAN trial

Massive Transfusion Protocol.rationale: Activate if EBL >1500mL or hemodynamically unstable

Shoulder Dystocia.pe_findings: Turtle sign (fetal head retracts against perineum), inability to deliver anterior shoulder with routine traction, maternal distress

McRoberts Maneuver.rationale: Sharply flex maternal thighs to abdomen; first-line intervention

Suprapubic Pressure.rationale: Continuous or rocking pressure above symphysis to dislodge shoulder

Rubin or Wood Screw.rationale: Internal rotation maneuvers if McRoberts + suprapubic fail

Deliver Posterior Arm.rationale: Sweep posterior arm across chest; last resort before Zavanelli

Ovarian Torsion.pe_findings: Sudden severe unilateral lower abdominal/pelvic pain, nausea/vomiting, adnexal tenderness, guarding, possible palpable mass

Pelvic Ultrasound.rationale: Doppler to assess ovarian blood flow; enlarged ovary with absent/decreased flow

GYN Consult.rationale: Emergent laparoscopy for detorsion; salvage ovary if viable

Pain Management.rationale: IV morphine or ketorolac; severe pain is the norm

Acetaminophen Overdose.pe_findings: Initially asymptomatic or mild nausea/vomiting (phase 1), then RUQ pain and liver tenderness (phase 2), jaundice and coagulopathy (phase 3)

APAP Level.rationale: 4-hour post-ingestion level; plot on Rumack-Matthew nomogram

NAC.rationale: N-acetylcysteine 150mg/kg IV over 1h, then 50mg/kg over 4h, then 100mg/kg over 16h; start if line above treatment threshold or unknown timing

Activated Charcoal.rationale: 1g/kg PO if within 1-2 hours of ingestion and airway protected

Organophosphate Poisoning.pe_findings: SLUDGE/DUMBELS: Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defecation, GI distress, Emesis; miosis, bradycardia, bronchorrhea, bronchospasm, muscle fasciculations

Atropine.rationale: 2mg IV q5min until secretions dry; may need large doses (10-20mg or more)

Pralidoxime.rationale: 1-2g IV over 15-30min; reactivates cholinesterase if given before aging

Decontamination.rationale: Remove clothing, wash skin with soap and water; protect healthcare workers with PPE

Intubation.rationale: If respiratory failure from bronchospasm/bronchorrhea; avoid succinylcholine

Carbon Monoxide Poisoning.pe_findings: Headache, confusion, cherry-red skin (late/unreliable), nausea, dizziness, syncope, SpO2 FALSELY NORMAL on pulse oximetry, tachycardia

100% O2 via NRB.rationale: Half-life of COHb drops from 5h to 90min on 100% O2; immediately start

CO-Oximetry.rationale: ABG with co-oximetry is the ONLY reliable test; pulse ox cannot detect COHb

Hyperbaric Oxygen.rationale: Consider if COHb >25%, loss of consciousness, cardiac ischemia, or pregnancy

Alcohol Withdrawal.pe_findings: Tremor, diaphoresis, agitation, tachycardia, hypertension, hallucinations (visual), seizures, delirium tremens if severe; CIWA score elevated

Benzodiazepine Protocol.rationale: Symptom-triggered dosing per CIWA: diazepam 10-20mg or lorazepam 2-4mg q1h PRN

Thiamine.rationale: 100mg IV BEFORE glucose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy

Electrolyte Repletion.rationale: Magnesium 2g IV, potassium and phosphorus PRN

Serotonin Syndrome.pe_findings: Clonus (especially lower extremity), hyperreflexia, agitation, diaphoresis, mydriasis, hyperthermia, tremor, muscle rigidity, diarrhea

Discontinue Serotonergic Agents.rationale: Stop all SSRIs, SNRIs, MAOIs, triptans, tramadol, linezolid, etc.

Cyproheptadine.rationale: 12mg PO initial, then 2mg q2h; serotonin antagonist; only available PO

Active Cooling.rationale: Target temp <38.5°C; ice packs, cooling blankets; avoid antipyretics (ineffective)

Benzodiazepines.rationale: Diazepam or lorazepam for agitation and muscle rigidity

Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome.pe_findings: Lead-pipe rigidity, hyperthermia (>40°C), altered mental status, autonomic instability (BP lability, tachycardia, diaphoresis), tremor

Dantrolene.rationale: 1-2.5mg/kg IV; muscle relaxant to reduce rigidity and thermogenesis

Active Cooling.rationale: Aggressive; target temp <38.5°C; ice packs, cooling blankets, cold IV fluids

Necrotizing Fasciitis.pe_findings: Pain out of proportion to exam, rapidly spreading erythema, crepitus, dusky/necrotic skin, bullae, systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia, hypotension)

Broad-Spectrum Antibiotics.rationale: Vancomycin + pip-tazo + clindamycin (toxin suppression); IV stat

Surgical Consult.rationale: EMERGENT surgical debridement is the definitive treatment; do not delay

Aggressive IV Fluids.rationale: These patients develop severe sepsis; 30mL/kg initial bolus

Pyelonephritis.pe_findings: CVA tenderness, fever, rigors, dysuria, suprapubic tenderness, nausea/vomiting, flank pain

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Ceftriaxone 1g IV or fluoroquinolone; broaden if septic

CT Abdomen/Pelvis.rationale: If no improvement in 48-72h; rule out abscess or obstruction

IV Fluids.rationale: Many patients volume-depleted from fever, vomiting, poor intake

Encephalitis.pe_findings: Altered mental status, fever, headache, seizures, focal neurological deficits, personality/behavioral changes, possible temporal lobe signs (HSV)

IV Acyclovir.rationale: 10mg/kg IV q8h; start empirically — do NOT wait for HSV PCR results

MRI Brain.rationale: Temporal lobe enhancement suggests HSV; more sensitive than CT

Lumbar Puncture.rationale: CSF analysis + HSV PCR; lymphocytic pleocytosis expected

Seizure Management.rationale: Levetiracetam or lorazepam; seizures common with HSV encephalitis

Sickle Cell Crisis.pe_findings: Severe bone pain (back, chest, extremities), tachycardia, possible splenic sequestration (LUQ mass), jaundice, ACS if chest involvement

Aggressive Pain Management.rationale: IV morphine or hydromorphone PCA; NSAIDs adjunct; do NOT undertreat pain

IV Fluids.rationale: NS at 1-1.5x maintenance; avoid overhydration (can precipitate ACS)

Transfusion.rationale: Simple or exchange transfusion if Hgb <6 or acute chest syndrome

Incentive Spirometry.rationale: Q2h while awake to prevent ACS; most critical preventive measure

DIC.pe_findings: Simultaneous bleeding (oozing from IV sites, petechiae, purpura, mucosal bleeding) AND thrombosis, organ dysfunction, acrocyanosis

Treat Underlying Cause.rationale: DIC is always secondary; identify and treat trigger (sepsis, malignancy, trauma, OB)

Blood Products.rationale: Platelets if <10K or bleeding; FFP if INR >1.5 and bleeding; cryo if fibrinogen <100

Heparin.rationale: Only if thrombosis predominates (chronic DIC); NOT if active hemorrhage

Neutropenic Fever.pe_findings: Fever with ANC <500, often minimal localizing signs (lack of inflammatory response), tachycardia, possible perianal tenderness, oral mucositis

Blood Cultures x2.rationale: Peripheral AND central line cultures before antibiotics

Empiric Antibiotics.rationale: Cefepime or meropenem monotherapy STAT per IDSA guidelines; do NOT delay

Chest X-Ray.rationale: May be falsely negative due to low WBC; CT if high suspicion

G-CSF Consideration.rationale: Filgrastim if expected prolonged neutropenia (>7 days)

Febrile Seizure.pe_findings: Generalized tonic-clonic activity in febrile child (6mo-5yr), post-ictal drowsiness, no focal deficits (if simple), fever source identified

Airway Positioning.rationale: Recovery position; suction PRN; do not restrain

Antipyretics.rationale: Acetaminophen 15mg/kg or ibuprofen 10mg/kg for fever control

Benzodiazepine.rationale: Midazolam 0.2mg/kg IM or diazepam 0.5mg/kg PR if seizure >5 min

Fever Source Workup.rationale: UA, CXR as indicated; LP if <12 months or complex features

Bronchiolitis.pe_findings: Wheezing and crackles, nasal flaring, intercostal/subcostal retractions, tachypnea, copious nasal secretions, poor feeding, cough

Nasal Suctioning.rationale: Bulb or deep suction to clear secretions; most effective intervention

Supplemental O2.rationale: Maintain SpO2 >90%; high-flow nasal cannula if moderate-severe

Hydration Assessment.rationale: IV or NG fluids if unable to maintain oral intake

Apnea Monitoring.rationale: Especially if <2 months, premature, or history of apnea

Kawasaki Disease.pe_findings: Fever >5 days, bilateral conjunctival injection, strawberry tongue, cervical lymphadenopathy, polymorphous rash, extremity changes (edema, erythema, desquamation)

IVIG.rationale: 2g/kg IV over 10-12 hours; first-line treatment; must give within 10 days of fever onset

High-Dose Aspirin.rationale: 80-100mg/kg/day divided q6h until afebrile; then 3-5mg/kg/day for 6-8 weeks

Echocardiogram.rationale: Baseline echo to assess coronary arteries; repeat at 2 and 6-8 weeks

Intussusception.pe_findings: Intermittent colicky abdominal pain (child draws knees to chest), vomiting, currant jelly stool (late), sausage-shaped mass in RUQ, lethargy between episodes

Abdominal Ultrasound.rationale: Target sign (donut sign) is diagnostic; sensitivity >95%

Air/Hydrostatic Enema.rationale: Therapeutic reduction by radiology; success rate 80-95%

Surgery Consult.rationale: Emergent if peritonitis, perforation, failed reduction, or pathologic lead point

IV Fluids.rationale: Maintenance + deficit replacement; these children are often dehydrated from vomiting

Neonatal Sepsis.pe_findings: Temperature instability (hypothermia more common than fever), poor feeding, lethargy, irritability, apnea, tachypnea, mottled skin, bulging fontanelle (if meningitis)

Blood Culture.rationale: Before antibiotics; minimum 1mL in pediatric bottle

Empiric Antibiotics.rationale: Ampicillin + gentamicin for early-onset (<7 days); add cefotaxime if meningitis suspected

Lumbar Puncture.rationale: CSF culture and analysis; defer only if hemodynamically unstable

Glucose Check.rationale: Neonates at high risk for hypoglycemia; D10W bolus 2mL/kg if low

Heat Stroke.pe_findings: Core temp >40°C (104°F), altered mental status, hot dry skin (classic) or diaphoresis (exertional), tachycardia, hypotension, possible seizures

Aggressive Cooling.rationale: Target <39°C within 30min; ice water immersion is gold standard; cold IV fluids, ice packs to groin/axillae/neck

Core Temperature Monitoring.rationale: Rectal or esophageal probe; tympanic/axillary unreliable

IV Fluids.rationale: NS or LR 1-2L bolus; most patients are severely volume depleted

Monitor for DIC.rationale: Check fibrinogen, PT/INR, platelets; DIC is major cause of death

Hypothermia.pe_findings: Core temp <35°C (95°F), bradycardia, Osborn (J) waves on ECG, AMS progressing to coma, shivering (mild) or absence of shivering (severe), muscle rigidity

Active Rewarming.rationale: Warm blankets + warm IV fluids (38-42°C) for mild; invasive rewarming for severe (<30°C)

Core Temperature Monitoring.rationale: Esophageal or rectal; monitor continuously during rewarming

Cardiac Monitoring.rationale: High risk of Vfib below 30°C; avoid rough handling; defibrillation may be ineffective until rewarmed

ECMO.rationale: For severe hypothermia with cardiac arrest; extracorporeal rewarming is most effective

Drowning.pe_findings: Respiratory distress or arrest, hypothermia, cyanosis, altered consciousness, frothy sputum, diffuse crackles, possible cardiac arrest

Rescue Breathing/CPR.rationale: Initiate immediately; ventilation is priority over compressions in drowning

Supplemental O2/Intubation.rationale: 100% O2; intubate if apneic, GCS <8, or refractory hypoxia; expect high PEEP needs

Rewarming.rationale: Active rewarming if hypothermic; continue resuscitation until rewarmed to 32°C

C-Spine Precautions.rationale: If mechanism suggests diving injury or unknown circumstances

Electrical Injury.pe_findings: Entry and exit wounds, cardiac arrhythmia, compartment syndrome, rhabdomyolysis, altered consciousness, tetanic muscle contraction marks, possible fractures from falls

12-Lead ECG.rationale: Arrhythmia screening; cardiac monitoring x 24h minimum

Aggressive IV Fluids.rationale: Target UOP 1-2mL/kg/hr to prevent myoglobinuric AKI

Compartment Pressure Checks.rationale: High risk for compartment syndrome; fasciotomy if pressures >30mmHg

Trauma Survey.rationale: Secondary injuries from fall or thrown mechanism; C-spine if applicable

Acute Psychosis.pe_findings: Agitation, paranoid delusions, auditory/visual hallucinations, disorganized speech, combative behavior, tachycardia from sympathetic activation

Verbal De-escalation.rationale: First-line; calm environment, empathic stance, offer choices

Chemical Sedation.rationale: Olanzapine 10mg IM or haloperidol 5mg + lorazepam 2mg IM (B52) if de-escalation fails

Medical Clearance.rationale: BMP, CBC, UA, tox screen, TSH; rule out organic causes (delirium, thyroid, infection)

STEMI - Anterior Wall.pe_findings: Diaphoresis, clutching chest, S4 gallop, possible crackles at bases, anxious appearance

12-Lead ECG.rationale: ST elevation in V1-V4; reciprocal changes in II, III, aVF

Cath Lab Activation.rationale: Door-to-balloon <90 min; call interventional cardiology STAT

STEMI - Inferior Wall.pe_findings: Bradycardia, diaphoresis, nausea/vomiting, possible RV involvement (JVD, clear lungs, hypotension)

12-Lead ECG.rationale: ST elevation in II, III, aVF; get right-sided leads for RV infarct

Right-Sided ECG.rationale: V4R to evaluate RV involvement — AVOID nitroglycerin and morphine if positive

STEMI - Lateral Wall.pe_findings: Left arm and jaw pain, diaphoresis, S4 gallop, anxious, possible mitral regurgitation murmur

12-Lead ECG.rationale: ST elevation in I, aVL, V5-V6; reciprocal ST depression in III, aVF

NSTEMI.pe_findings: Substernal pressure, diaphoresis, possible radiation to arm/jaw, anxious, S4 gallop possible

12-Lead ECG.rationale: ST depression and/or T-wave inversion; NO ST elevation

Serial Troponins.rationale: Repeat at 3 and 6 hours to document rise/fall pattern

Anticoagulation.rationale: Heparin drip + aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor per ACS protocol

Risk Stratification.rationale: TIMI or GRACE score to determine early invasive vs conservative strategy

Prinzmetal Angina.pe_findings: Chest pain at rest (often early morning), transient ST elevation that resolves with NTG, normal exam between episodes

12-Lead ECG During Pain.rationale: Transient ST elevation during episode; normalizes with resolution

Nitroglycerin.rationale: SL NTG for acute relief; IV NTG drip if recurrent

Calcium Channel Blocker.rationale: Diltiazem or amlodipine; mainstay of prevention. AVOID beta-blockers (can worsen spasm)

Ventricular Tachycardia - Stable.pe_findings: Wide-complex tachycardia on monitor, palpitations, lightheadedness, diaphoresis, still conscious and oriented

12-Lead ECG.rationale: Wide QRS >120ms, AV dissociation, fusion/capture beats confirm VT over SVT with aberrancy

Amiodarone.rationale: 150mg IV over 10 min, then 1mg/min drip x 6h; first-line for stable monomorphic VT

Defibrillator at Bedside.rationale: Pads on patient; can decompensate to VFib without warning

Electrolyte Correction.rationale: Correct K+ to >4.0 and Mg2+ to >2.0

Ventricular Tachycardia - Unstable.pe_findings: Wide-complex tachycardia, altered mental status, diaphoresis, weak pulse, near-syncope or syncope, possible chest pain

Synchronized Cardioversion.rationale: 100-200J biphasic STAT; do NOT delay for amiodarone if unstable

Sedation.rationale: Brief sedation with etomidate or midazolam if patient is conscious

Post-Cardioversion 12-Lead.rationale: Assess underlying rhythm and rule out STEMI

Ventricular Fibrillation.pe_findings: Pulseless, unresponsive, chaotic rhythm on monitor, no detectable blood pressure, agonal/absent respirations

Defibrillation.rationale: Immediate unsynchronized shock 120-200J biphasic; every 2 min as needed

High-Quality CPR.rationale: Begin immediately; 100-120 compressions/min, 2-inch depth, full recoil

Amiodarone.rationale: 300mg IV/IO first dose after 3rd shock; 150mg for second dose

Torsades de Pointes.pe_findings: Polymorphic VT with twisting QRS axis, pulselessness or near-syncope, preceded by prolonged QT, possible syncope

IV Magnesium.rationale: 2g MgSO4 IV over 2 min; first-line treatment regardless of Mg level

Defibrillation.rationale: Unsynchronized shock if pulseless; may self-terminate and recur

Overdrive Pacing.rationale: Temporary pacing at 90-110 bpm to suppress pause-dependent TdP

Atrial Flutter.pe_findings: Regular tachycardia (often 150 bpm suggesting 2:1 block), sawtooth flutter waves on ECG, palpitations, mild dyspnea

12-Lead ECG.rationale: Sawtooth pattern in II, III, aVF; rate often 150 bpm (2:1 block) or 100 (3:1)

Rate Control.rationale: IV diltiazem 0.25mg/kg or metoprolol; target HR <110

Anticoagulation Assessment.rationale: CHA2DS2-VASc score; anticoagulate if score ≥2 per guidelines

Second-Degree Heart Block Type I (Wencke.pe_findings: Gradually prolonging PR interval with dropped beat, usually asymptomatic or mild lightheadedness, regular irregularity

12-Lead ECG.rationale: Progressive PR prolongation before dropped QRS; grouped beating pattern

Monitoring.rationale: Usually benign; observe for progression to higher-degree block

Second-Degree Heart Block Type II (Mobit.pe_findings: Sudden dropped QRS without PR prolongation, possible syncope, fatigue, exercise intolerance, wide QRS baseline

12-Lead ECG.rationale: Fixed PR with sudden dropped QRS; infranodal block; wide QRS typical

Transcutaneous Pacing.rationale: Pads on and ready; high risk of progressing to complete heart block

Cardiology Consult.rationale: Permanent pacemaker indicated for all symptomatic Mobitz II

Atropine.rationale: May worsen infranodal block; use with caution; TCP preferred

Sick Sinus Syndrome.pe_findings: Alternating bradycardia and tachycardia (tachy-brady syndrome), syncope, fatigue, dizziness, exercise intolerance

12-Lead ECG + Telemetry.rationale: Sinus bradycardia, sinus pauses, or tachy-brady with paroxysmal AFib/flutter

Atropine.rationale: 0.5mg IV for symptomatic bradycardia as bridge

Pacemaker Evaluation.rationale: Permanent pacemaker is definitive treatment for symptomatic SSS

Wolff-Parkinson-White Syndrome.pe_findings: Extremely rapid rate, delta wave on baseline ECG, short PR interval, wide QRS during tachycardia, possible hemodynamic instability

12-Lead ECG.rationale: Delta wave, short PR (<120ms), wide QRS; during tachycardia: irregularly irregular wide complex if AFib

Procainamide.rationale: First-line for stable WPW with AFib; 20-50mg/min IV. AVOID AV nodal blockers

Avoid AV Nodal Blockers.rationale: NO adenosine, diltiazem, verapamil, digoxin — can cause VFib via unopposed accessory pathway conduction

Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia.pe_findings: Irregular rhythm with ≥3 distinct P-wave morphologies, commonly associated with COPD or pulmonary disease, mild dyspnea

12-Lead ECG.rationale: ≥3 different P-wave morphologies, varying PR intervals, irregular rate >100

Magnesium Repletion.rationale: IV MgSO4 2g; often curative; most patients are Mg-depleted

Treat Underlying Cause.rationale: Optimize COPD/pulmonary disease, correct hypoxia, fix electrolytes

Junctional Rhythm.pe_findings: Regular narrow-complex rhythm at 40-60 bpm, absent or retrograde P waves, lightheadedness, fatigue

12-Lead ECG.rationale: Narrow QRS, rate 40-60, absent/inverted P waves before or after QRS

Atropine.rationale: 0.5mg IV if symptomatic; assess for reversible causes

Drug Review.rationale: Check for digoxin toxicity, beta-blocker or CCB excess

Accelerated Idioventricular Rhythm.pe_findings: Wide-complex regular rhythm at 60-100 bpm, often seen post-reperfusion MI, usually hemodynamically stable

12-Lead ECG.rationale: Wide QRS, regular rate 60-100; often reperfusion arrhythmia post-PCI/thrombolysis

Observation.rationale: Usually benign and self-limiting; do NOT treat with antiarrhythmics unless symptomatic

Monitor for Degeneration.rationale: Watch for acceleration to VT; keep defibrillator at bedside

Acute Mitral Regurgitation.pe_findings: Acute pulmonary edema, new holosystolic murmur at apex radiating to axilla, bilateral crackles, JVD, S3 gallop, tachycardia

Bedside Echo.rationale: Severe MR with flail leaflet or papillary muscle rupture; LA/LV dilation

Vasodilator Therapy.rationale: Nitroprusside or NTG drip to reduce afterload; IABP if hemodynamically unstable

Emergent Surgery Consult.rationale: Mitral valve repair/replacement for acute papillary muscle rupture

Acute Aortic Regurgitation.pe_findings: Acute pulmonary edema, wide pulse pressure, early diastolic murmur (may be soft), bilateral crackles, tachycardia, Austin Flint murmur possible

Bedside Echo.rationale: Severe AR, premature mitral valve closure, LV volume overload

Vasodilator Therapy.rationale: Nitroprusside to reduce afterload; AVOID beta-blockers (remove compensatory tachycardia)

Emergent Surgery Consult.rationale: Aortic valve replacement; medical therapy is temporizing only

Aortic Stenosis - Decompensated.pe_findings: Crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at RUSB radiating to carotids, syncope or near-syncope, exertional dyspnea, pulsus parvus et tardus, S4 gallop

Bedside Echo.rationale: Aortic valve area, mean gradient, LVEF; AVA <1.0 cm² = severe

Cautious Volume Management.rationale: Avoid aggressive vasodilation or volume depletion — preload dependent

Cardiology/CT Surgery Consult.rationale: TAVR vs SAVR evaluation; medical management bridges only

Mitral Stenosis - Decompensated.pe_findings: Low-pitched diastolic rumble at apex with opening snap, AFib commonly coexistent, bilateral crackles, hemoptysis possible, JVD, malar flush

12-Lead ECG.rationale: AFib common; P mitrale if in sinus; RVH possible

Rate Control.rationale: Slow heart rate to prolong diastolic filling time; IV beta-blocker preferred

Diuresis.rationale: IV furosemide for pulmonary congestion; careful not to reduce preload excessively

Echo.rationale: Mitral valve area, gradient, LA size, pulmonary pressures

Prosthetic Valve Thrombosis.pe_findings: Mechanical click absent or diminished, new murmur, acute heart failure symptoms, subtherapeutic INR history, dyspnea at rest

TEE.rationale: Transesophageal echo to assess thrombus size and valve function

Heparin Drip.rationale: Unfractionated heparin for immediate anticoagulation; target aPTT 60-80s

Thrombolysis vs Surgery.rationale: tPA for left-sided valve if surgical risk high or thrombus <1cm; surgery for large thrombus

Infective Endocarditis.pe_findings: New or changing murmur, fever, splinter hemorrhages, Janeway lesions, Osler nodes, Roth spots on fundoscopy, splenomegaly, petechiae

Blood Cultures x3.rationale: 3 sets from separate sites before antibiotics; 30 min apart

Empiric Antibiotics.rationale: Vancomycin + ceftriaxone for native valve; vancomycin + gentamicin + rifampin for prosthetic

TTE/TEE.rationale: TEE superior for vegetations; assess size, valve destruction, abscess

CT Surgery Consult.rationale: For large vegetations >10mm, heart failure, abscess, or embolic events

Acute Limb Ischemia.pe_findings: 6 P's: Pain, Pallor, Pulselessness, Paresthesias, Paralysis, Poikilothermia; cool pale extremity, absent distal pulses, mottling

Heparin Bolus.rationale: 80 units/kg IV bolus then 18 units/kg/hr drip; prevent clot propagation

Vascular Surgery Consult.rationale: Emergent for embolectomy, thrombectomy, or bypass

CTA or Angiography.rationale: Define location and extent of occlusion

Pain Management.rationale: IV opioids; limb ischemia is extremely painful

Deep Vein Thrombosis.pe_findings: Unilateral leg swelling, calf tenderness, warmth, erythema, positive Homans sign (unreliable), pitting edema, distended superficial veins

Duplex Ultrasound.rationale: Compression US of symptomatic leg; non-compressibility confirms DVT

Anticoagulation.rationale: Enoxaparin 1mg/kg BID or rivaroxaban 15mg BID; bridge to warfarin or continue DOAC

PE Assessment.rationale: Monitor for dyspnea, tachycardia, chest pain suggesting PE

Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm Rupture.pe_findings: Sudden severe abdominal/back pain, pulsatile abdominal mass, hemodynamic instability, Grey Turner sign, flank ecchymosis, syncope

Large-Bore IV x2.rationale: Two 14-16g IVs; crossmatch for 6+ units immediately

Permissive Hypotension.rationale: Target SBP 70-80 until surgical repair; avoid over-resuscitation

Emergent Vascular Surgery.rationale: Open repair or EVAR — this is a surgical emergency

Thoracic Aortic Aneurysm Rupture.pe_findings: Sudden tearing chest/back pain, hemodynamic collapse, mediastinal widening on CXR, possible hemothorax, muffled heart sounds if hemopericardium

CT Angiography.rationale: If stable enough; defines anatomy for surgical planning

Emergent CT Surgery.rationale: Open repair required for ascending aortic rupture

Peripheral Arterial Disease - Critical L.pe_findings: Rest pain in foot (worse with elevation), non-healing ulcers, gangrene, absent pedal pulses, ABI <0.4, dependent rubor, pallor on elevation

Vascular Surgery Consult.rationale: Revascularization vs amputation; angiography for planning

Pain Management.rationale: Dependent positioning; opioids for rest pain

Wound Care.rationale: Offloading, infection assessment, debridement if needed

Acute Decompensated Heart Failure.pe_findings: Bilateral crackles, JVD, peripheral edema, S3 gallop, orthopnea, PND, weight gain, hepatojugular reflux

IV Diuretics.rationale: Furosemide 40-80mg IV (or 2.5x home dose); target UOP 0.5-1mL/kg/hr

Nitroglycerin.rationale: SL or IV drip for afterload reduction if SBP >110; NOT if hypotensive

BiPAP.rationale: Non-invasive ventilation for work of breathing; reduces preload and afterload

Cardiology Consult.rationale: Optimize guideline-directed medical therapy; consider advanced therapies

Flash Pulmonary Edema.pe_findings: Acute severe dyspnea, pink frothy sputum, bilateral crackles throughout, diaphoresis, severe hypertension, tripod positioning, cyanosis

IV Nitroglycerin.rationale: High-dose NTG drip 200-400mcg/min; most effective intervention for hypertensive flash edema

BiPAP.rationale: Immediate NIPPV; CPAP 10-12 cmH2O or BiPAP; dramatically reduces intubation rates

IV Furosemide.rationale: 80mg IV; onset slower than NTG but necessary for volume removal

Intubation.rationale: If BiPAP fails or GCS declines; RSI with etomidate or ketamine

Right Heart Failure.pe_findings: JVD, hepatomegaly, ascites, peripheral edema, RV heave, loud P2, tricuspid regurgitation murmur, hepatojugular reflux, clear lungs (no pulmonary edema)

Volume Assessment.rationale: Careful — RV failure is preload dependent; avoid aggressive diuresis

Treat Underlying Cause.rationale: PE, pulmonary hypertension, RV infarct; treatment differs dramatically

Inotropic Support.rationale: Milrinone or dobutamine for severe RV failure; reduce RV afterload

Acute Pericarditis.pe_findings: Pleuritic chest pain worse when supine, improved leaning forward, pericardial friction rub (scratchy 3-component), diffuse ST elevation with PR depression on ECG

12-Lead ECG.rationale: Diffuse concave-up ST elevation, PR depression; Spodick sign (downsloping TP segment)

NSAID Therapy.rationale: Ibuprofen 600mg TID or ASA 650mg TID + colchicine 0.5mg BID for 3 months

Echo.rationale: Assess for pericardial effusion; most cases are small or absent

Constrictive Pericarditis.pe_findings: JVD with Kussmaul sign (JVD increases with inspiration), pericardial knock, hepatomegaly, ascites, peripheral edema, exertional dyspnea

Echo.rationale: Septal bounce, respirophasic variation, thickened pericardium

Cardiac Catheterization.rationale: Equalization of diastolic pressures across all 4 chambers

CT Surgery Consult.rationale: Pericardiectomy is definitive treatment for severe cases

Hypertensive Emergency - Aortic Dissecti.pe_findings: Tearing interscapular pain, BP differential >20mmHg between arms, wide mediastinum on CXR, aortic regurgitation murmur possible, diaphoresis

IV Esmolol.rationale: Target HR <60 FIRST, then target SBP 100-120; esmolol 500mcg/kg bolus then 50-200mcg/kg/min

CT Angiography.rationale: Confirm dissection and classify Stanford Type A vs B

Arterial Line.rationale: Continuous BP monitoring essential for safe titration

CT Surgery Consult.rationale: Emergent repair for Type A; medical management ± TEVAR for Type B

Hypertensive Encephalopathy.pe_findings: Severe headache, altered mental status, visual changes (blurred vision, cortical blindness), papilledema, nausea/vomiting, seizures possible

IV Nicardipine.rationale: 5mg/hr, titrate q5min by 2.5mg/hr; target 20-25% MAP reduction in first hour

CT Head.rationale: Rule out hemorrhagic stroke; PRES may show posterior white matter edema on MRI

Arterial Line.rationale: Continuous BP monitoring for safe titration

Seizure Prophylaxis.rationale: Levetiracetam if seizures present; lorazepam for acute seizure

Distributive Shock - Anaphylactic.pe_findings: Hypotension, tachycardia, urticaria, angioedema, wheezing/stridor, warm flushed skin, abdominal cramping

Epinephrine IM.rationale: 0.3-0.5mg IM (1:1000) in anterolateral thigh; repeat q5-15min; FIRST-LINE always

Aggressive IV Fluids.rationale: NS 1-2L bolus; massive third-spacing occurs in anaphylaxis

Airway Assessment.rationale: Early intubation if stridor, tongue swelling, or voice changes — airway edema can progress rapidly

Epinephrine Drip.rationale: If refractory: 1-10 mcg/min IV drip; switch from 1:1000 IM to 1:10000 IV

Obstructive Shock - Tension Pneumothorax.pe_findings: Tracheal deviation away from affected side, absent breath sounds unilaterally, JVD, hypotension, tachycardia, hyperresonance to percussion

Needle Decompression.rationale: 14g needle 2nd ICS MCL or 5th ICS AAL; immediate lifesaving intervention

Chest Tube.rationale: Definitive management after needle decompression; 28-32 Fr tube thoracostomy

CXR.rationale: Post-procedure to confirm lung re-expansion; NOT needed before decompression if clinical diagnosis

Obstructive Shock - Massive PE.pe_findings: Acute dyspnea, hypoxemia, hypotension, JVD, RV strain on ECG (S1Q3T3, RBBB), possible PEA arrest

Bedside Echo.rationale: RV dilation, McConnell sign, D-shaped septum; can diagnose in minutes

Systemic Thrombolysis.rationale: Alteplase 100mg IV over 2h for massive PE with hemodynamic instability; accept bleeding risk

Heparin.rationale: UFH 80 units/kg bolus then 18 units/kg/hr; start while awaiting imaging

Neurogenic Shock.pe_findings: Hypotension + bradycardia + warm dry extremities (loss of sympathetic tone), spinal cord injury history, flaccid paralysis below level, priapism

IV Fluids.rationale: Cautious fluid resuscitation first; avoid over-resuscitation

Vasopressors.rationale: Norepinephrine or phenylephrine to maintain MAP >85 for 7 days per AANS guidelines

Atropine.rationale: For symptomatic bradycardia; keep at bedside

Takotsubo Cardiomyopathy.pe_findings: Chest pain mimicking ACS, ST elevation or T-wave inversion, often following emotional/physical stress, apical ballooning on echo, mild HF symptoms

12-Lead ECG.rationale: ST elevation or deep T-wave inversions; may mimic anterior STEMI

Cath Lab.rationale: Normal coronaries with apical ballooning on ventriculogram; diagnostic

Supportive Care.rationale: ACE inhibitor + beta-blocker; most recover fully in 4-8 weeks

Monitor for Cardiogenic Shock.rationale: 5-10% develop severe LV dysfunction or LVOT obstruction

Myocarditis.pe_findings: Chest pain (often pleuritic), preceding viral illness, tachycardia out of proportion to fever, S3 gallop, possible heart failure signs, pericardial friction rub

12-Lead ECG.rationale: Diffuse ST changes, low voltage, possible arrhythmias; may mimic STEMI

Echo.rationale: Wall motion abnormalities, reduced EF, possible pericardial effusion

Cardiac MRI.rationale: Gold standard for diagnosis; late gadolinium enhancement in non-coronary pattern

Heart Failure Management.rationale: ACE inhibitor + beta-blocker + diuretics PRN; avoid NSAIDs in acute phase

Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy Crisis.pe_findings: Dynamic LVOT obstruction with harsh crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur (increases with Valsalva), syncope, chest pain, dyspnea, possible sudden cardiac death

IV Fluids.rationale: Volume loading to increase preload and reduce LVOT gradient

IV Phenylephrine.rationale: Pure alpha agonist increases afterload; reduces obstruction. AVOID dobutamine, milrinone, NTG

Beta-Blocker.rationale: IV esmolol to reduce HR and contractility; decreases LVOT gradient

Avoid Vasodilators.rationale: NO nitroglycerin, NO diuretics, NO inotropes — all worsen obstruction

Cardiac Contusion.pe_findings: Chest wall tenderness post-blunt trauma, steering wheel injury, arrhythmias on monitor, possible sternal fracture, anterior chest ecchymosis

12-Lead ECG.rationale: New arrhythmias, ST changes, RBBB; monitor continuously for 24-48h

Echo.rationale: Assess wall motion abnormalities, pericardial effusion, valve injury

24h Telemetry.rationale: Continuous monitoring for late arrhythmias; most significant in first 24h

Pacemaker Malfunction.pe_findings: Syncope or presyncope, bradycardia, pacing spikes without capture, pocket swelling or erythema if infection, possible hiccups (diaphragmatic pacing)

12-Lead ECG.rationale: Assess pacing spikes: failure to capture, failure to sense, or failure to output

Magnet Application.rationale: Place magnet over pacer to force asynchronous pacing (VOO/DOO mode)

Transcutaneous Pacing.rationale: External TCP if magnet fails to restore pacing; bridge to EP consult

EP/Cardiology Consult.rationale: Generator replacement, lead revision, or reprogramming

ICD Storm.pe_findings: Multiple ICD shocks (≥3 in 24h), recurrent VT/VF, anxiety, diaphoresis, pain from repeated shocks, possible underlying ischemia

IV Amiodarone.rationale: 150mg IV over 10 min then 1mg/min drip; suppress recurrent VT

Electrolyte Correction.rationale: Target K >4.0, Mg >2.0; often triggers for VT storm

Sedation/Anxiolysis.rationale: Midazolam or propofol; repeated shocks are traumatizing

EP Consult.rationale: May need VT ablation, ICD reprogramming, or deep sedation with intubation

Commotio Cordis.pe_findings: Sudden cardiac arrest in young athlete after blunt chest impact (baseball, hockey puck), VFib on monitor, no structural heart disease

Defibrillation.rationale: Immediate shock for VFib; survival inversely related to time to defibrillation

ACLS Protocol.rationale: Standard VFib arrest protocol; structurally normal heart expected

Post-Arrest Echo.rationale: Confirm normal cardiac anatomy; rule out HCM or other structural disease

Pulmonary Hypertension Crisis.pe_findings: Severe dyspnea, RV failure signs (JVD, hepatomegaly, edema), loud P2, RV heave, tricuspid regurgitation, cyanosis, possible syncope

Supplemental O2.rationale: Target SpO2 >90%; hypoxia worsens pulmonary vasoconstriction

Inhaled Nitric Oxide.rationale: Selective pulmonary vasodilator; 20-40 ppm; optimal for right heart rescue

IV Epoprostenol.rationale: If inhaled NO unavailable; start at 2ng/kg/min and titrate

Avoid Intubation If Possible.rationale: Positive pressure ventilation can precipitate RV collapse; use NIPPV first

Cardiac Arrest - PEA.pe_findings: Pulseless with organized electrical activity on monitor, no pulse with CPR check, consider H's and T's for reversible causes

High-Quality CPR.rationale: Begin immediately; 100-120/min, full recoil, minimize interruptions

Epinephrine.rationale: 1mg IV/IO q3-5min; start immediately for non-shockable rhythm

H's and T's Workup.rationale: Hypovolemia, Hypoxia, H+, Hypo/Hyperkalemia, Hypothermia; Tension pneumo, Tamponade, Toxins, Thrombosis

Bedside Echo.rationale: Assess during pulse check — identify tamponade, RV strain (PE), hypovolemia

Cardiac Arrest - Asystole.pe_findings: Pulseless, no electrical activity on monitor (confirm in ≥2 leads to rule out fine VFib), agonal or absent respirations

High-Quality CPR.rationale: Begin immediately; do NOT defibrillate asystole

Epinephrine.rationale: 1mg IV/IO q3-5min; start immediately for non-shockable rhythm

Confirm Asystole.rationale: Check ≥2 leads; check connections; rule out fine VFib

Reversible Causes.rationale: Aggressive search for H's and T's; asystole carries worst prognosis

Post-Cardiac Arrest Syndrome.pe_findings: Post-ROSC: hemodynamic instability, neurological impairment (GCS variable), reperfusion injury, multi-organ dysfunction, possible rearrest

Targeted Temperature Management.rationale: Target 32-36°C for 24h; cooling initiated ASAP post-ROSC per AHA guidelines

Hemodynamic Optimization.rationale: Target MAP >65; vasopressors (norepinephrine) and fluids as needed

12-Lead ECG.rationale: Assess for STEMI — emergent cath lab if ST elevation regardless of consciousness

Neuroprognostication.rationale: Defer ≥72h post-rewarming; use multimodal approach (exam, EEG, SSEP, MRI)

Brugada Syndrome.pe_findings: Syncope (especially at rest or during sleep), family history of sudden death, type 1 Brugada pattern on ECG (coved ST elevation V1-V3), Asian male predominant

12-Lead ECG.rationale: Type 1 pattern: coved ST elevation ≥2mm in V1-V3 with RBBB morphology

Continuous Telemetry.rationale: Monitor for VT/VF; most events occur during sleep or rest

EP Consult.rationale: ICD implantation for secondary prevention (survived arrest) or high-risk features

Avoid Triggers.rationale: No class Ia/Ic antiarrhythmics, no fever (antipyretics aggressively), avoid sodium channel blockers

Long QT Syndrome.pe_findings: Syncope (especially exercise-related in LQT1, auditory trigger in LQT2, sleep in LQT3), family history of sudden death, prolonged QTc (>500ms)

12-Lead ECG.rationale: QTc >500ms high risk; assess T-wave morphology for genotype; check for TdP/VF

Medication Review.rationale: Discontinue ALL QT-prolonging drugs (crediblemeds.org); common offenders: antiemetics, antibiotics, antipsychotics

Beta-Blocker Therapy.rationale: Nadolol or propranolol for LQT1/LQT2; mexiletine for LQT3

ICD Evaluation.rationale: For survivors of cardiac arrest; breakthrough events on beta-blockers; QTc >500 with syncope

Aortic Coarctation - Adult Presentation.pe_findings: Upper extremity hypertension with lower extremity hypotension, BP differential >20mmHg, radial-femoral pulse delay, rib notching on CXR, continuous murmur

4-Extremity BP.rationale: ≥20mmHg gradient between upper and lower extremities confirms coarctation

CT Angiography.rationale: Define anatomy and severity of coarctation; associated bicuspid aortic valve

BP Control.rationale: Beta-blocker first-line; avoid pure vasodilators that increase gradient

Cardiology/CT Surgery Consult.rationale: Balloon angioplasty with stenting or surgical repair for significant gradient

Acute Limb Ischemia - Embolic.pe_findings: Sudden onset 6 P's (Pain, Pallor, Pulselessness, Paresthesias, Paralysis, Poikilothermia), sharp demarcation, history of AFib or recent MI, contralateral pulses normal

Emergent Embolectomy.rationale: Fogarty catheter embolectomy; time is tissue

Compartment Assessment.rationale: Post-reperfusion compartment syndrome risk; fasciotomy if pressures >30mmHg

Source Identification.rationale: Echo for cardiac thrombus; AFib management

Mesenteric Venous Thrombosis.pe_findings: Subacute abdominal pain (days to weeks), distension, nausea, possible bloody stool, peritoneal signs if infarction, history of hypercoagulable state

CT Angiography.rationale: Venous phase CTA shows filling defect in SMV/portal vein

Anticoagulation.rationale: Heparin drip; mainstay of treatment for non-operative MVT

Surgery Consult.rationale: For peritonitis, pneumatosis, or portal venous gas suggesting bowel infarction

Hypercoagulability Workup.rationale: Protein C/S, antithrombin, Factor V Leiden, antiphospholipid antibodies

Aortitis.pe_findings: Back/chest pain, fever, elevated inflammatory markers, possible aortic regurgitation, limb claudication if branch vessel involvement, bruits

CT Angiography.rationale: Aortic wall thickening and enhancement; assess for aneurysm

High-Dose Steroids.rationale: Prednisone 1mg/kg or methylprednisolone for giant cell arteritis/Takayasu

Temporal Artery Biopsy.rationale: If GCA suspected; treat while awaiting biopsy results

Superior Vena Cava Syndrome.pe_findings: Facial/neck swelling, JVD, dilated chest wall veins (collateral circulation), Pemberton sign, cyanosis of face, dyspnea worse when supine, headache

CT Chest with Contrast.rationale: Identify mass, thrombus, or compression of SVC; determine etiology

Elevate HOB.rationale: 30-45 degrees to reduce venous pressure and facial edema

Oncology/IR Consult.rationale: SVC stenting for severe symptoms; radiation or chemo for malignant etiology

Acute Asthma Exacerbation - Mild/Moderat.pe_findings: Diffuse expiratory wheezing, prolonged expiratory phase, accessory muscle use, speaking in full sentences, tachypnea

Albuterol Nebulizer.rationale: 2.5mg neb q20min x3, then q1-4h PRN; continuous neb for severe

Peak Flow Monitoring.rationale: Serial PEFR to assess response; target >70% predicted for discharge

Status Asthmaticus.pe_findings: Severe wheezing progressing to silent chest, unable to speak, diaphoresis, accessory muscle use, paradoxical breathing, obtunded if fatigue

Continuous Albuterol.rationale: 10-20mg/hr continuous nebulization; do NOT use metered-dose inhaler

IV Magnesium.rationale: 2g MgSO4 IV over 20 min; bronchial smooth muscle relaxation

Intubation Preparation.rationale: Prepare for RSI with ketamine (bronchodilator); beware peri-intubation arrest from hyperinflation

COPD Exacerbation - Mild.pe_findings: Increased dyspnea/cough/sputum production, diffuse wheezing and rhonchi, barrel chest, pursed-lip breathing, mild accessory muscle use

Bronchodilators.rationale: Albuterol + ipratropium neb q4-6h; short-acting bronchodilator relief

Systemic Steroids.rationale: Prednisone 40mg PO daily x5 days (GOLD guidelines)

Antibiotics.rationale: Azithromycin or doxycycline if increased sputum purulence or severity

Titrated O2.rationale: Target SpO2 88-92%; avoid hyperoxia causing CO2 narcosis

COPD Exacerbation - Severe with Respirat.pe_findings: Severe dyspnea, tripod positioning, accessory muscle use, paradoxical abdominal breathing, cyanosis, altered mental status, minimal air movement

BiPAP.rationale: IPAP 12-20, EPAP 5-8; first-line for COPD with acute hypercapnic respiratory failure; reduces intubation by 50%

Intubation.rationale: If BiPAP fails, GCS declining, or hemodynamic instability; use low tidal volume, long expiratory time

Blood Gas Monitoring.rationale: Repeat ABG after 1h of BiPAP to assess response

Bronchiectasis Exacerbation.pe_findings: Chronic productive cough with purulent sputum (>200mL/day), coarse crackles, clubbing, hemoptysis possible, foul-smelling sputum

Sputum Culture.rationale: Culture before antibiotics; Pseudomonas common in severe bronchiectasis

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Anti-pseudomonal coverage (piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, or meropenem) if severe

Chest Physiotherapy.rationale: Airway clearance techniques; postural drainage; Flutter/Acapella valve

CT Chest.rationale: HRCT to assess extent; signet ring sign, tram-track sign

Community-Acquired Pneumonia - Moderate.pe_findings: Productive cough, focal crackles and rhonchi, decreased breath sounds, dullness to percussion, egophony, tactile fremitus increased

Blood Cultures.rationale: 2 sets before antibiotics for hospitalized patients

Empiric Antibiotics.rationale: Ceftriaxone 1g IV + azithromycin 500mg IV per ATS/IDSA guidelines

CXR.rationale: Confirm infiltrate; lobar consolidation vs interstitial pattern

CURB-65 Assessment.rationale: Risk stratify: Confusion, Urea, RR, BP, Age ≥65; score ≥2 = admit

Hospital-Acquired Pneumonia (HAP).pe_findings: New or worsening cough, purulent sputum, focal crackles, fever >48h after admission, leukocytosis, new infiltrate on CXR

Respiratory Cultures.rationale: Before antibiotics: sputum or BAL; cover MDR pathogens

Broad-Spectrum Antibiotics.rationale: Piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem ± vancomycin for MRSA risk per local antibiogram

De-escalation Plan.rationale: Narrow antibiotics based on culture results within 48-72h

Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia (VAP).pe_findings: New infiltrate on CXR ≥48h after intubation, purulent secretions, worsening oxygenation/ventilation, fever, leukocytosis

Empiric Antibiotics.rationale: Double-cover gram negatives ± MRSA coverage based on risk; meropenem + vancomycin + antipseudomonal

Ventilator Bundle Review.rationale: HOB elevation, daily sedation vacation, SBT assessment, DVT and stress ulcer prophylaxis

Lung Abscess.pe_findings: Foul-smelling purulent sputum, night sweats, weight loss, fever, crackles over abscess, possible amphoric breath sounds, clubbing if chronic

CT Chest.rationale: Thick-walled cavity with air-fluid level; typically in posterior upper lobes or superior lower lobes

Prolonged Antibiotics.rationale: Ampicillin-sulbactam or clindamycin + fluoroquinolone; 4-6 weeks typical course

Sputum Cultures.rationale: Anaerobic and aerobic cultures; mixed flora common

IR Drainage.rationale: Percutaneous drainage if >6cm or failing medical therapy after 7-10 days

Aspiration Pneumonia.pe_findings: Witnessed aspiration event or risk factors (altered consciousness, dysphagia), rhonchi/crackles in dependent lung zones, foul-smelling sputum

CXR.rationale: Infiltrate in dependent segments — RLL (upright) or posterior upper lobes (supine)

Antibiotics.rationale: Ampicillin-sulbactam or clindamycin for anaerobic coverage; NOT needed for aspiration pneumonitis alone

Aspiration Precautions.rationale: HOB >30°, NPO, swallow eval if recurrent

Suctioning.rationale: Clear airway of visible aspirate if intubated

Empyema.pe_findings: Persistent fever despite antibiotics for pneumonia, decreased breath sounds with dullness to percussion, reduced chest expansion, pleuritic pain

CT Chest.rationale: Loculated pleural effusion with enhancing pleural thickening (split pleura sign)

Thoracentesis.rationale: Diagnostic: pH <7.20, glucose <60, LDH >3x serum, positive Gram stain/culture

Chest Tube Drainage.rationale: Large-bore tube for frank pus; consider tPA/DNase if loculated

VATS.rationale: If chest tube drainage inadequate after 72h; thoracic surgery consult

Spontaneous Pneumothorax - Primary.pe_findings: Sudden pleuritic chest pain, decreased breath sounds unilaterally, hyperresonance to percussion, typically tall thin young male

CXR.rationale: Visceral pleural line with absent lung markings peripherally; expiratory film may help

Needle Aspiration.rationale: For first episode >2cm on CXR; 16-18g needle 2nd ICS MCL

Chest Tube.rationale: If aspiration fails or large/symptomatic; 14-16 Fr anterior or pigtail catheter

High-Flow O2.rationale: 100% O2 increases reabsorption rate 4x for small pneumothorax managed conservatively

Spontaneous Pneumothorax - Secondary.pe_findings: Dyspnea out of proportion to pneumothorax size (limited reserve), absent breath sounds, underlying lung disease (COPD, CF), cyanosis possible

CXR.rationale: Confirm pneumothorax; may be harder to see with underlying emphysema

Chest Tube.rationale: All secondary pneumothorax >1cm or symptomatic require chest tube; NOT aspiration alone

Thoracic Surgery Consult.rationale: VATS with pleurodesis for recurrent; higher recurrence rate than primary

Hemothorax.pe_findings: Decreased breath sounds with dullness to percussion, signs of hemorrhage (tachycardia, hypotension), chest wall trauma, deviated trachea if massive

Chest Tube.rationale: 32-36 Fr tube thoracostomy for drainage and autotransfusion assessment

Massive Transfusion.rationale: If initial output >1500mL or ongoing >200mL/hr for 2-4h

Thoracotomy.rationale: If >1500mL initial or >200mL/hr x4h; trauma surgery consult

CXR.rationale: Post-tube placement to assess drainage and lung re-expansion

Malignant Pleural Effusion.pe_findings: Progressive dyspnea, decreased breath sounds at base, dullness to percussion, weight loss, cachexia, known or suspected malignancy

Thoracentesis.rationale: Therapeutic AND diagnostic; exudative (Light's criteria); send cytology

Indwelling Pleural Catheter.rationale: For recurrent symptomatic effusion; outpatient drainage

CT Chest.rationale: Assess underlying tumor, pleural nodularity, adenopathy

Oncology Consult.rationale: Systemic therapy, pleurodesis, or palliative care discussion

Chylothorax.pe_findings: Dyspnea, milky white pleural fluid, post-surgical (thoracic/esophageal) or traumatic, non-pleuritic, weight loss

Thoracentesis.rationale: Milky fluid; triglycerides >110mg/dL diagnostic; chylomicrons on lipoprotein analysis

NPO + TPN or MCT Diet.rationale: Reduce chyle output; MCT are absorbed directly into portal system, bypassing lymphatics

Thoracic Duct Ligation.rationale: If output >1L/day for >5 days or nutritional depletion

ARDS - Mild.pe_findings: Acute onset bilateral infiltrates, tachypnea, dyspnea, hypoxemia refractory to supplemental O2, crackles bilaterally, no evidence of cardiogenic cause

CXR.rationale: Bilateral opacities not fully explained by effusions or atelectasis

High-Flow Nasal Cannula.rationale: HFNC at 40-60 LPM; may avoid intubation in mild ARDS

Treat Underlying Cause.rationale: Sepsis, pneumonia, aspiration — address the trigger

Serial ABGs.rationale: Monitor P/F ratio; worsening suggests progression

ARDS - Moderate/Severe.pe_findings: Severe hypoxemia (P/F <150), bilateral white-out on CXR, stiff lungs on ventilator, high FiO2 requirement, refractory to recruitment

Low Tidal Volume Ventilation.rationale: ARDSNet protocol: Vt 4-6 mL/kg IBW, plateau pressure ≤30 cmH2O

Prone Positioning.rationale: 16+ hours/day for P/F <150; proven mortality benefit (PROSEVA trial)

Neuromuscular Blockade.rationale: Cisatracurium for first 48h if P/F <150; consider early in severe ARDS

Conservative Fluid Strategy.rationale: Target CVP <4 or PAOP <8; avoid fluid overload

Acute Respiratory Failure - Hypoxemic (T.pe_findings: Acute dyspnea, tachypnea, cyanosis, accessory muscle use, variable lung exam depending on cause, PaO2 <60 on room air

ABG.rationale: Confirm type 1 (hypoxemic) failure: PaO2 <60, PaCO2 normal or low

Identify Etiology.rationale: CXR, CT, echo — pneumonia, PE, ARDS, pneumothorax, pulmonary edema

Escalate O2 Support.rationale: NC → HFNC → BiPAP → intubation based on response

Treat Underlying Cause.rationale: Antibiotics, anticoagulation, drainage, etc. based on etiology

Acute Respiratory Failure - Hypercapnic.pe_findings: Somnolence, confusion, shallow rapid breathing or bradypnea, asterixis, warm flushed skin (CO2 vasodilation), headache, papilledema possible

ABG.rationale: Confirm type 2: PaCO2 >50 with acidosis; acute vs chronic (bicarb compensation)

BiPAP.rationale: First-line: IPAP 12-20, EPAP 5-8; addresses ventilatory failure directly

Controlled O2.rationale: Target SpO2 88-92%; do NOT overcorrect oxygen — drives CO2 retention

Intubation.rationale: If BiPAP fails, GCS <8, or hemodynamic instability

Submassive Pulmonary Embolism.pe_findings: Dyspnea, tachycardia, pleuritic chest pain, RV strain on echo but hemodynamically stable, possible DVT signs

CTA Chest.rationale: Central or lobar PE; assess RV/LV ratio >0.9 = RV strain

Bedside Echo.rationale: RV dilation, McConnell sign, TAPSE <16mm = significant RV strain

Heparin Drip.rationale: UFH 80 units/kg bolus + 18 units/kg/hr; therapeutic anticoagulation

Risk Stratify for Escalation.rationale: PESI score + troponin + RV function; consider catheter-directed therapy if high risk

Saddle Pulmonary Embolism.pe_findings: Acute dyspnea, near-syncope, severe hypoxemia, RV failure (JVD, RV heave), possible PEA arrest, saddle embolus at bifurcation

Systemic Thrombolysis.rationale: Alteplase 100mg IV over 2h if hemodynamically unstable; mortality benefit

Bedside Echo.rationale: Massive RV dilation; can diagnose and trigger thrombolysis before CTA

Heparin.rationale: Start immediately; do NOT delay for imaging if clinical suspicion high

Surgical Embolectomy.rationale: If thrombolysis contraindicated or failed; ECMO bridge

Pulmonary Infarction.pe_findings: Pleuritic chest pain, hemoptysis, low-grade fever, pleural friction rub, wedge-shaped peripheral opacity on CXR (Hampton hump)

CTA Chest.rationale: Segmental/subsegmental PE with peripheral wedge-shaped consolidation

Anticoagulation.rationale: Standard anticoagulation for PE; additional management for infarction is supportive

Pain Management.rationale: NSAIDs or opioids for pleuritic pain; facilitates deep breathing

Fat Embolism Syndrome.pe_findings: Classic triad: respiratory distress + neurological changes + petechial rash (conjunctiva, axillae, chest) 24-72h post-long bone fracture, ARDS-like picture

Supportive Care.rationale: No specific treatment; supportive respiratory care is mainstay

Mechanical Ventilation.rationale: ARDSNet protocol if intubated; lung-protective ventilation

Early Fracture Fixation.rationale: Reduces ongoing fat embolization; orthopedic consult

Serial Monitoring.rationale: Neurological checks, platelet trends, SpO2; usually self-limiting 72h

Air Embolism.pe_findings: Sudden cardiovascular collapse during central line placement/removal, mill wheel murmur on auscultation, acute dyspnea, may gasp, altered consciousness

Left Lateral Decubitus + Trendelenburg.rationale: Durant maneuver: left side down, head down; traps air in RV apex away from RVOT

Aspiration via Central Line.rationale: If multi-orifice RA catheter in place, aspirate air directly

Hyperbaric Oxygen.rationale: If available and cerebral air embolism suspected; reduces bubble size

Epiglottitis - Adult.pe_findings: Severe sore throat, muffled 'hot potato' voice, drooling, tripod positioning, stridor, dysphagia, fever, thumbprint sign on lateral neck XR

Airway Management.rationale: ENT + anesthesia at bedside; fiberoptic intubation preferred; surgical airway backup ready

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Ceftriaxone 2g IV + vancomycin for H. influenzae, Strep, Staph coverage

Avoid Agitation.rationale: NO tongue depressors, NO blind oropharyngeal exam — can precipitate complete obstruction

Angioedema - Hereditary.pe_findings: Non-pitting, non-pruritic swelling of face/lips/tongue/larynx, NO urticaria (distinguishes from allergic), positive family history, abdominal pain attacks

C1-Esterase Inhibitor Concentrate.rationale: Berinert 20 units/kg IV; first-line for HAE attacks

Icatibant.rationale: Bradykinin B2 receptor antagonist SQ; alternative to C1-INH concentrate

Airway Management.rationale: Early intubation if tongue/laryngeal involvement; can progress rapidly

Epinephrine/Antihistamines NOT Effective.rationale: Bradykinin-mediated, NOT histamine; standard allergy drugs do not work

Angioedema - ACE Inhibitor Induced.pe_findings: Lip/tongue/uvula swelling in patient on ACE inhibitor, no urticaria, may occur after years on medication, African American patients at higher risk

Discontinue ACE Inhibitor.rationale: Immediately and permanently; switch to ARB with caution (small cross-reactivity risk)

Airway Assessment.rationale: Fiberoptic visualization of larynx if tongue/floor of mouth involved

Icatibant or Fresh Frozen Plasma.rationale: Off-label but emerging evidence for bradykinin-mediated angioedema

Observation 24h.rationale: Can worsen over 24-48h; monitor closely even after improvement

Foreign Body Aspiration - Adult.pe_findings: Acute choking episode then persistent cough/wheeze, unilateral wheezing or decreased breath sounds, possible history of elderly/neurological impairment eating

Back Blows + Abdominal Thrusts.rationale: If complete obstruction and conscious; Heimlich maneuver

Rigid Bronchoscopy.rationale: Definitive removal for tracheal/mainstem objects; performed under general anesthesia

Flexible Bronchoscopy.rationale: For distal objects; can visualize and extract with basket/forceps

CXR.rationale: May show radiopaque object, unilateral hyperinflation (ball-valve effect), or atelectasis

Vocal Cord Dysfunction.pe_findings: Inspiratory stridor/wheeze (NOT expiratory), 'can't get air in', throat tightness, episodes precipitated by stress/exercise, often misdiagnosed as asthma

Laryngoscopy During Attack.rationale: Paradoxical vocal cord adduction on inspiration; posterior chink visible

Reassurance + Breathing Techniques.rationale: Panting, sniffing, pursed-lip breathing; resolves most episodes

Heliox.rationale: 80/20 helium-oxygen mixture reduces work of breathing through narrowed airway

Speech Therapy Referral.rationale: Laryngeal control techniques; definitive long-term management

Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis Exacerbati.pe_findings: Rapid worsening of dyspnea over days to weeks in known IPF, bilateral velcro crackles at bases, clubbing, new ground-glass opacities on CT

High-Flow O2 or BiPAP.rationale: Supplemental O2; HFNC preferred; intubation has very poor prognosis

CT Chest.rationale: New bilateral GGOs superimposed on UIP pattern

Broad-Spectrum Antibiotics.rationale: Cover for infection as trigger; empiric until cultures return

Palliative Care Discussion.rationale: AE-IPF has >50% in-hospital mortality; goals of care essential

Sarcoidosis - Pulmonary.pe_findings: Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy on CXR, dry cough, dyspnea, erythema nodosum, anterior uveitis, hypercalcemia possible, young African American

CXR.rationale: Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy (Stage I/II) ± parenchymal infiltrates

Biopsy.rationale: Non-caseating granulomas; transbronchial biopsy or lymph node sampling
