Corticosteroids.rationale: Prednisone 20-40mg/day for symptomatic pulmonary disease; not all stages require treatment

Eosinophilic Pneumonia - Acute.pe_findings: Acute febrile illness with severe hypoxemia, bilateral infiltrates mimicking ARDS, recent new drug/smoke exposure, peripheral eosinophilia may be ABSENT initially

BAL.rationale: BAL eosinophils >25% diagnostic; peripheral eosinophilia may lag

IV Methylprednisolone.rationale: 125mg IV q6h then taper; dramatic and rapid response is characteristic

CT Chest.rationale: Bilateral peripheral or random GGOs; pleural effusions common

Pulmonary Alveolar Hemorrhage.pe_findings: Hemoptysis (may be absent in 1/3), dyspnea, bilateral alveolar infiltrates, dropping hemoglobin, often in setting of vasculitis or anti-GBM disease

Bronchoscopy.rationale: Sequentially bloodier BAL aliquots confirm DAH; hemosiderin-laden macrophages

Pulse Steroids.rationale: Methylprednisolone 1g IV daily x3 days; then high-dose oral taper

Plasmapheresis.rationale: For anti-GBM disease or severe ANCA vasculitis with DAH

Cyclophosphamide.rationale: Rituximab as alternative; immunosuppression for underlying vasculitis

Massive Hemoptysis.pe_findings: Coughing large volumes of blood (>100-600mL/24h), may see bleeding source lateralized, potential airway compromise, possible hemodynamic instability

Position Bleeding Side Down.rationale: Lateral decubitus with affected lung dependent to protect good lung

Intubation.rationale: Large ETT (≥8.0) for bronchoscale; consider selective main bronchus intubation to isolate bleeding

IR Bronchial Artery Embolization.rationale: First-line definitive therapy; >90% success for bronchial artery source

Rigid Bronchoscopy.rationale: Allows airway control + visualization; can tamponade with balloon

Obstructive Sleep Apnea - Acute Decompen.pe_findings: Obesity, somnolence, morning headaches, witnessed apneic episodes, polycythemia, cor pulmonale signs (JVD, edema), Mallampati IV

BiPAP.rationale: Preferredover CPAP for hypercapnic respiratory failure; IPAP 14-20, EPAP 6-10

Controlled O2.rationale: Target SpO2 88-92%; avoid overcorrection in chronic hypercapnia

Weight-Based Dosing Caution.rationale: Avoid sedatives, opioids; exquisitely sensitive to respiratory depression

Polysomnography Outpatient.rationale: Formal sleep study for CPAP titration after acute stabilization

Obesity Hypoventilation Syndrome.pe_findings: BMI >30 with chronic daytime hypercapnia, somnolence, morning headaches, polycythemia, cor pulmonale, Pickwickian habitus

BiPAP.rationale: Average volume-assured pressure support (AVAPS) or standard BiPAP; mainstay of treatment

ABG Monitoring.rationale: Serial ABGs to guide ventilatory support; target PaCO2 improvement

Diuretics.rationale: For cor pulmonale and volume overload; furosemide cautiously

Weight Loss Referral.rationale: Bariatric surgery evaluation for BMI >40 or >35 with comorbidities

Pulmonary Contusion.pe_findings: Post-blunt chest trauma, progressive dyspnea over 24-48h, hemoptysis, crackles over affected area, chest wall ecchymosis, adjacent rib fractures

CT Chest.rationale: Ground-glass opacity/consolidation not respecting fissures; evolves over 24-48h

Lung-Protective Ventilation.rationale: If intubated: ARDSNet protocol; contusion can progress to ARDS

Fluid Restriction.rationale: Judicious fluids; avoid overhydration which worsens contusion edema

Pain Management.rationale: Rib blocks, epidural, or multimodal analgesia; incentive spirometry

Flail Chest.pe_findings: Paradoxical chest wall movement (segment moves inward on inspiration), severe chest wall pain, crepitus, ≥3 consecutive ribs fractured in ≥2 places, splinting

Aggressive Pain Control.rationale: Epidural or intercostal nerve blocks preferred; IV opioids; adequate analgesia prevents atelectasis

Intubation + PPV.rationale: Positive pressure ventilation acts as internal splint; for respiratory failure

CT Chest.rationale: Full assessment of fractures, underlying pulmonary contusion, hemothorax

Surgical Fixation.rationale: Rib plating for severe flail; reduces ventilator days and ICU stay

Tracheobronchial Injury.pe_findings: Massive subcutaneous emphysema, pneumomediastinum, persistent air leak after chest tube, hemoptysis, voice change if laryngeal, Hamman sign

Bronchoscopy.rationale: Diagnostic: visualize tear; flexible for distal, rigid for proximal/if hemorrhage

Selective Intubation.rationale: Advance ETT past tear under bronchoscopic guidance if needed

Chest Tube.rationale: For pneumothorax; persistent large air leak suggests major airway injury

Thoracic Surgery Consult.rationale: Operative repair for tears >1/3 circumference or deteriorating

Smoke Inhalation Injury.pe_findings: Singed nasal hairs, carbonaceous sputum, hoarseness, oropharyngeal erythema/edema, wheezing, stridor, facial burns, enclosed-space fire history

100% FiO2.rationale: High-flow O2; CO half-life reduced from 5h to 1h on 100% O2; pulse ox unreliable with CO

Early Intubation.rationale: If ANY signs of upper airway involvement — edema rapidly progresses; intubate before swelling worsens

Bronchoscopy.rationale: Assess severity of lower airway injury; soot, erythema, edema below cords

Hydroxocobalamin.rationale: 5g IV for suspected cyanide toxicity (enclosed space fire); empiric treatment

Near-Drowning / Submersion Injury.pe_findings: Pulmonary edema, hypothermia, altered consciousness, frothy sputum, diffuse crackles, variable GCS, possible cardiac arrest

100% FiO2.rationale: Immediate high-flow O2; non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema from surfactant washout

Rewarming.rationale: Active rewarming for hypothermia; warm IV fluids, Bair hugger, warm humidified O2

Intubation.rationale: For GCS <8, severe hypoxemia, or hemodynamic instability

C-Spine Precautions.rationale: If diving or mechanism suggestive of cervical injury

Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury (T.pe_findings: Acute dyspnea, bilateral infiltrates, and hypoxemia within 6h of blood transfusion, no evidence of circulatory overload, non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema

Stop Transfusion.rationale: Immediately discontinue the implicated blood product

Supportive Care.rationale: O2, ventilatory support as needed; diuretics NOT helpful (non-cardiogenic)

Report to Blood Bank.rationale: Transfusion reaction workup; donor antibody testing

Mechanical Ventilation.rationale: ARDSNet protocol if required; usually resolves in 48-96h

Transfusion-Associated Circulatory Overl.pe_findings: Dyspnea, hypertension, JVD, bilateral crackles during or shortly after transfusion, elevated BNP (distinguishes from TRALI), peripheral edema

Stop or Slow Transfusion.rationale: Stop immediately if severe; slow rate if mild

IV Furosemide.rationale: 40-80mg IV; true volume overload responds to diuresis

Future Transfusion Planning.rationale: Slower rates, concurrent diuretics, consider single-unit transfusions

Central Sleep Apnea - Cheyne-Stokes.pe_findings: Crescendo-decrescendo breathing pattern with central apneas, typically in heart failure, daytime somnolence, disrupted sleep, periodic desaturations on oximetry

Optimize Heart Failure.rationale: Treat underlying HF; CSA may resolve with GDMT optimization

Adaptive Servo-Ventilation.rationale: AVOID in EF <45% (SERVE-HF trial showed harm); use for EF >45%

Supplemental O2.rationale: Nocturnal O2 reduces AHI; improves desaturations

Polysomnography.rationale: Formal diagnosis and differentiation from obstructive events

Laryngospasm.pe_findings: Complete or near-complete airway obstruction, stridor, paradoxical chest movement, occurs post-extubation or with airway irritation, unable to ventilate

100% FiO2 with CPAP.rationale: Jaw thrust + firm continuous positive pressure may break laryngospasm

Succinylcholine.rationale: 0.1-0.5mg/kg IV or 4mg/kg IM if complete obstruction unresponsive to CPAP

Intubation.rationale: If laryngospasm persists after succinylcholine; prepare for difficult airway

Larson Maneuver.rationale: Firm pressure in laryngospasm notch (behind lobule of each ear); may break spasm

Ischemic Stroke - MCA.pe_findings: Contralateral hemiparesis (face/arm>leg), aphasia if dominant hemisphere, hemineglect if non-dominant, gaze deviation toward lesion

CT Head.rationale: Non-contrast CT to rule out hemorrhage before tPA

tPA Administration.rationale: Alteplase 0.9mg/kg IV within 4.5h of symptom onset per AHA/ASA guidelines

NIH Stroke Scale.rationale: Quantify deficit severity; guides treatment decisions

Thrombectomy Eval.rationale: LVO on CTA → mechanical thrombectomy up to 24h per DAWN/DEFUSE3

Ischemic Stroke - Posterior Circulation.pe_findings: Vertigo, diplopia, dysarthria, ataxia, crossed findings (ipsilateral cranial nerve + contralateral motor), possible locked-in syndrome

CT Head.rationale: Non-contrast CT; may miss early posterior fossa infarcts

MRI Brain.rationale: DWI most sensitive for posterior circulation stroke

tPA.rationale: Same window as anterior circulation if no contraindications

Lacunar Stroke.pe_findings: Pure motor hemiparesis OR pure sensory loss OR ataxic hemiparesis OR dysarthria-clumsy hand; no cortical signs (no aphasia, neglect, visual field cuts)

MRI Brain.rationale: DWI to confirm small deep infarct

Hemorrhagic Stroke - ICH.pe_findings: Sudden headache, vomiting, progressive focal deficit, decreased consciousness, possible seizure; signs reflect location (basal ganglia, thalamus, cerebellum, pons)

BP Control.rationale: Target SBP <140 per INTERACT2; IV nicardipine or clevidipine

Coagulopathy Reversal.rationale: PCC for warfarin, idarucizumab for dabigatran, andexanet for Xa inhibitors

Neurosurgery Consult.rationale: For cerebellar ICH >3cm, hydrocephalus, or herniation

Cerebellar Stroke.pe_findings: Sudden vertigo, vomiting, severe ataxia, dysarthria, nystagmus, inability to walk; DANGER: rapid deterioration from brainstem compression or obstructive hydrocephalus

CT Head.rationale: Emergent; evaluate posterior fossa and fourth ventricle

Neurosurgery Consult.rationale: Emergent for decompressive craniectomy or EVD if hydrocephalus

Serial Neuro Checks.rationale: Q15min — rapid deterioration is hallmark of cerebellar stroke

Transient Ischemic Attack.pe_findings: Resolved focal neurological deficit (was present <24h), normal exam now; ABCD2 score determines risk of subsequent stroke

MRI Brain.rationale: DWI to rule out completed infarct

Dual Antiplatelet.rationale: ASA + clopidogrel for 21 days if ABCD2 ≥4 per POINT trial

Cardiac Monitoring.rationale: Telemetry x 24h minimum; extended if AF suspected

Subdural Hematoma - Acute.pe_findings: Progressive headache, contralateral hemiparesis, ipsilateral pupil dilation, declining consciousness; crescent-shaped hyperdensity on CT crossing suture lines

Neurosurgery Consult.rationale: Craniotomy for SDH >10mm or midline shift >5mm

ICP Management.rationale: HOB 30°, mannitol or hypertonic saline if herniation signs

Subdural Hematoma - Chronic.pe_findings: Insidious headache weeks after minor trauma, cognitive decline, gait instability, subtle hemiparesis; elderly on anticoagulants; crescent-shaped iso/hypodensity on CT

CT Head.rationale: Iso or hypodense crescent; may have mixed density if rebleed

Neurosurgery Consult.rationale: Burr hole drainage for symptomatic cases

Cerebral Venous Thrombosis.pe_findings: Headache (often progressive over days), papilledema, focal deficits, seizures; consider in young women on OCPs, postpartum, or with hypercoagulable states

CT Venogram or MRV.rationale: Gold standard; empty delta sign on contrast CT

Anticoagulation.rationale: Heparin drip even with hemorrhagic infarction per AHA guidelines

Seizure Management.rationale: Levetiracetam if seizures; CVT has high seizure incidence

Brain Abscess.pe_findings: Headache, fever, focal neurological deficit, seizures; triad present in <50% of cases; ring-enhancing lesion on CT/MRI

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Empiric: ceftriaxone + metronidazole +/- vancomycin; 6-8 week course

Neurosurgery Consult.rationale: Aspiration/excision if >2.5cm or causing mass effect

Meningitis - Bacterial.pe_findings: Fever, headache, nuchal rigidity, photophobia, altered mental status, positive Kernig and Brudzinski signs, petechial rash if meningococcal

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Vancomycin + ceftriaxone STAT; do NOT delay for LP if unstable

Dexamethasone.rationale: 0.15mg/kg IV before or with first antibiotic dose; reduces mortality in pneumococcal

Lumbar Puncture.rationale: After CT if indicated; opening pressure, cell count, protein, glucose, gram stain, culture

Blood Cultures.rationale: Before antibiotics if possible; but NEVER delay antibiotics for cultures

Meningitis - Viral.pe_findings: Headache, fever, mild nuchal rigidity, photophobia; generally less toxic-appearing than bacterial; normal mental status typically preserved

Lumbar Puncture.rationale: Lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal glucose distinguishes from bacterial

HSV PCR.rationale: Send on all viral meningitis CSF; acyclovir if HSV suspected

Supportive Care.rationale: IV fluids, analgesics, antiemetics; most cases self-limited

Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus.pe_findings: Classic triad: gait apraxia (magnetic gait), urinary incontinence, dementia; 'wet, wacky, wobbly'; gait abnormality typically earliest and most prominent

CT/MRI Brain.rationale: Ventriculomegaly out of proportion to sulcal atrophy

Large Volume LP.rationale: Remove 30-50mL CSF; assess gait improvement post-tap (positive predictor for shunt)

Myasthenia Gravis Crisis.pe_findings: Rapidly progressive weakness, respiratory failure, dyspnea, weak cough, dysphagia, ptosis, diplopia, nasal speech; NIF worsening

NIF/VC Monitoring.rationale: NIF <-20 or VC <1L or declining → intubate; check q2h

IVIG or Plasmapheresis.rationale: Both equally effective; IVIG 0.4g/kg/day x 5 days

Hold Cholinesterase Inhibitors.rationale: May worsen secretions and confound respiratory assessment in crisis

Multiple Sclerosis - Acute Relapse.pe_findings: New or worsening neurological symptoms >24h: optic neuritis (painful vision loss), transverse myelitis, brainstem syndrome; Uhthoff phenomenon (heat worsening)

MRI Brain/Spine.rationale: Gadolinium-enhancing lesions confirm acute inflammation; dissemination in space and time

IV Methylprednisolone.rationale: 1g IV daily x 3-5 days; hastens recovery from relapse

Trigeminal Neuralgia.pe_findings: Sudden severe lancinating facial pain in V2/V3 distribution, triggered by chewing/touching/wind; unilateral; pain-free intervals between attacks

MRI Brain.rationale: Rule out structural cause: tumor, MS plaque, vascular compression

Carbamazepine.rationale: 200mg BID titrate up; first-line treatment; check Na and CBC at baseline

Pain Management.rationale: Acute: IV phenytoin or lidocaine infusion for status trigeminus

Bell Palsy.pe_findings: Acute unilateral facial paralysis involving forehead (LMN pattern); difficulty closing eye, drooping mouth, loss of nasolabial fold; hyperacusis, taste changes

CT/MRI.rationale: Only if atypical features suggesting stroke or tumor; not routine

Prednisone.rationale: 60-80mg/day x 7 days with taper; best if started within 72h of onset

Eye Protection.rationale: Artificial tears, eye patch at night to prevent corneal exposure keratopathy

Cauda Equina Syndrome.pe_findings: Low back pain, bilateral leg weakness, saddle anesthesia, bowel/bladder dysfunction (urinary retention most sensitive), decreased rectal tone, bilateral absent ankle reflexes

MRI Lumbar Spine.rationale: EMERGENT; compression of cauda equina nerve roots

Neurosurgery Consult.rationale: Emergent surgical decompression within 24-48h for best outcomes

Foley Catheter.rationale: Urinary retention is common and may require catheterization

Wernicke Encephalopathy.pe_findings: Classic triad (all three present in <33%): confusion/AMS, ophthalmoplegia (lateral rectus palsy, nystagmus), gait ataxia; malnourished/alcoholic patient

IV Thiamine.rationale: 500mg IV TID x 3 days; give BEFORE glucose to prevent precipitation of Wernicke

Magnesium Repletion.rationale: Thiamine cannot be utilized without adequate magnesium

Glucose.rationale: Only AFTER thiamine administration; D50 if hypoglycemic

Central Cord Syndrome.pe_findings: Upper extremity weakness greater than lower (cape distribution), bladder dysfunction, variable sensory loss below level; typically hyperextension injury in elderly with cervical spondylosis

C-Spine MRI.rationale: Emergent to evaluate cord compression and edema

C-Spine Immobilization.rationale: Immediate; rigid collar until imaging clears

MAP Target.rationale: Maintain MAP >85 for spinal cord perfusion per AANS

Neurosurgery Consult.rationale: Surgical decompression if significant cord compression

Anterior Cord Syndrome.pe_findings: Bilateral motor paralysis and loss of pain/temperature below lesion level; preserved proprioception and vibration (posterior columns spared); worst prognosis of incomplete SCI syndromes

MRI Spine.rationale: Emergent; evaluate anterior spinal artery territory infarction vs compression

MAP Target.rationale: MAP >85 mmHg with pressors if needed

Surgical Consult.rationale: Consider emergent decompression if compressive etiology

Brown-Sequard Syndrome.pe_findings: Hemisection pattern: ipsilateral motor loss and proprioception loss, contralateral pain/temperature loss below lesion level; best prognosis of incomplete SCI syndromes

MRI Spine.rationale: Confirm hemisection pattern; evaluate for penetrating vs blunt etiology

Cerebral Aneurysm - Unruptured Symptomat.pe_findings: Sentinel headache (warning leak), cranial nerve palsy (CN III with posterior communicating aneurysm → ptosis, mydriasis), visual symptoms

Neurosurgery/Neurointerventional Consult.rationale: Discuss clipping vs coiling based on aneurysm characteristics

BP Control.rationale: Avoid hypertension; target SBP <140 given rupture risk

Admission.rationale: Close monitoring; any new headache could represent rupture

Hydrocephalus - Acute.pe_findings: Headache, vomiting, papilledema, upgaze palsy (setting sun sign), Cushing response (late), decreased consciousness; causes: tumor obstructing CSF, post-hemorrhagic, post-infectious

CT Head.rationale: Emergent; ventriculomegaly, periventricular edema, identify obstructive cause

EVD Placement.rationale: Emergent external ventricular drain by neurosurgery

ICP Management.rationale: HOB 30°, mannitol or 23.4% saline for acute herniation

Neurosurgery Consult.rationale: Emergent for EVD; definitive VP shunt or ETV later

Cerebral Edema.pe_findings: Progressive obtundation, pupil asymmetry, posturing (decorticate→decerebrate), Cushing triad, cerebral herniation syndromes (uncal, central, tonsillar)

Hyperosmolar Therapy.rationale: Mannitol 1g/kg or 23.4% NaCl 30mL for acute herniation

Intubation.rationale: Target PaCO2 35-40; brief hyperventilation to 30-35 ONLY for active herniation

CT Head.rationale: Identify cause and degree of shift

Neurosurgery Consult.rationale: Decompressive craniectomy if refractory; EVD for hydrocephalus

Pseudotumor Cerebri.pe_findings: Headache (worse with Valsalva, positional), papilledema, visual obscurations, pulsatile tinnitus, CN VI palsy; obese woman of childbearing age typical

MRI/MRV Brain.rationale: Rule out venous sinus thrombosis and structural causes; empty sella, tortuous optic nerves

Lumbar Puncture.rationale: Diagnostic AND therapeutic; elevated opening pressure with normal CSF composition

Acetazolamide.rationale: 250-500mg BID; reduces CSF production; first-line medical therapy

Ophthalmology Consult.rationale: Formal visual field testing; monitor for progressive visual loss

Myasthenic Crisis vs Cholinergic Crisis.pe_findings: Respiratory failure + weakness; DIFFERENTIATING: Myasthenic=dry, dilated pupils, worse with activity; Cholinergic=SLUDGE (salivation, lacrimation, urination, diarrhea, miosis), excess secretions

NIF/VC.rationale: NIF <-20 or VC <15mL/kg → intubate; trending is key

Edrophonium Test.rationale: 2mg test dose → if improves = myasthenic crisis; if worsens = cholinergic crisis (rarely done now)

IVIG or Plasmapheresis.rationale: Start definitive immunotherapy once myasthenic crisis confirmed

Spinal Epidural Abscess.pe_findings: Progressive back pain, fever, focal neurological deficit; classic triad: fever + spinal pain + neuro deficit (present <15% of cases); IVDU and diabetes are major risk factors

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Vancomycin + cefepime or meropenem; MRSA coverage essential

Neurosurgery Consult.rationale: Emergent surgical drainage if neurological deficits present or progressing

Blood Cultures.rationale: Before antibiotics; Staph aureus most common organism

Acute Disseminated Encephalomyelitis.pe_findings: Polyfocal neurological deficits days after viral illness or vaccination; encephalopathy, bilateral optic neuritis, transverse myelitis, cerebellar signs; predominantly pediatric

MRI Brain/Spine.rationale: Large, bilateral, fluffy white matter lesions; may enhance with gadolinium

Tension Headache.pe_findings: Bilateral pressing/tightening headache, mild-moderate intensity, not aggravated by physical activity; no nausea/photophobia/phonophobia (≤1 of these); pericranial tenderness

Clinical Diagnosis.rationale: History-based; no imaging needed unless red flags present

Analgesics.rationale: NSAIDs or acetaminophen first-line; avoid opioids and barbiturates

Red Flag Assessment.rationale: Thunderclap onset, fever, focal deficits, papilledema → further workup

Migraine - Acute Severe.pe_findings: Unilateral throbbing headache, nausea/vomiting, photophobia, phonophobia, worse with activity; may have aura (visual most common); status migrainosus if >72h

IV Antiemetic.rationale: Metoclopramide 10mg or prochlorperazine 10mg IV; dual action as both antiemetic and abortive

IV Ketorolac.rationale: 30mg IV; effective abortive; avoid in renal disease

IV Magnesium.rationale: 2g IV over 15min especially if with aura

CT Head.rationale: Only if first/worst headache, thunderclap onset, focal deficits, or red flags

Cluster Headache - Acute.pe_findings: Severe unilateral periorbital pain, ipsilateral lacrimation, conjunctival injection, ptosis, miosis, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea; patient restless/agitated (opposite of migraine)

High-Flow Oxygen.rationale: 100% O2 at 12-15 L/min via NRB x 15min; first-line treatment; 78% response rate

Subcutaneous Sumatriptan.rationale: 6mg SC; fastest pharmacologic abortive; contraindicated in CAD

Verapamil.rationale: First-line preventive during cluster period; 240-960mg/day with ECG monitoring

Seizure - New Onset.pe_findings: Witnessed tonic-clonic activity, post-ictal confusion and drowsiness, tongue laceration, incontinence; postictal Todd paralysis may mimic stroke

CT Head.rationale: Non-contrast to rule out structural/hemorrhagic cause

BMP.rationale: Electrolyte derangement workup: Na, Ca, Mg, glucose

EEG.rationale: Routine outpatient if first unprovoked seizure; urgent if concern for NCSE

Non-Convulsive Status Epilepticus.pe_findings: Altered mental status without obvious motor seizure activity; subtle signs: eye deviation, nystagmus, facial twitching, automatisms; diagnosis REQUIRES EEG

Continuous EEG.rationale: ONLY way to diagnose NCSE; should be obtained urgently for unexplained AMS

Benzodiazepine Trial.rationale: Lorazepam 0.1mg/kg IV; clinical and EEG improvement confirms diagnosis

Metabolic Workup.rationale: BMP, ammonia, hepatic panel, tox screen; rule out mimics

Antiepileptic Loading.rationale: Levetiracetam 60mg/kg or valproate 40mg/kg IV after benzo trial

Temporal Arteritis.pe_findings: New headache in patient >50, temporal artery tenderness with decreased pulsation, jaw claudication, visual symptoms (amaurosis fugax → permanent blindness), scalp tenderness, PMR symptoms

High-Dose Prednisone.rationale: 1mg/kg/day (max 60mg); START IMMEDIATELY — do NOT wait for biopsy; visual loss is irreversible

Temporal Artery Biopsy.rationale: Within 2 weeks of steroid start; skip lesions → need adequate length (>1cm)

Ophthalmology Consult.rationale: Urgent if visual symptoms; fundoscopy for optic disc pallor

IV Methylprednisolone.rationale: 1g IV x 3 days if visual loss already present; then oral prednisone

Autonomic Dysreflexia.pe_findings: In SCI patients at T6 or above: severe paroxysmal hypertension, reflex bradycardia, pounding headache, flushing/sweating above lesion level, piloerection, nasal congestion

Sit Patient Up.rationale: Immediately sit patient upright to use orthostatic effects to lower BP

Identify Noxious Stimulus.rationale: Most common: bladder distension (blocked catheter) → straight cath or flush; fecal impaction → disimpact with lidocaine jelly

Nifedipine.rationale: 10mg bite-and-swallow or nitropaste 1 inch above lesion if SBP >150 after stimulus removed

Loosen Clothing.rationale: Remove any tight clothing, abdominal binders, compression stockings below level of injury

Posterior Reversible Encephalopathy Synd.pe_findings: Headache, altered mental status, seizures, visual disturbances (cortical blindness), associated with severe hypertension, eclampsia, immunosuppressants, renal failure

MRI Brain.rationale: FLAIR hyperintensity in posterior parieto-occipital white matter; DWI typically negative (vasogenic not cytotoxic edema)

BP Control.rationale: Gradual reduction; target 25% reduction in first few hours

Seizure Management.rationale: Levetiracetam or lorazepam; magnesium if eclampsia-related

Remove Offending Agent.rationale: Stop cyclosporine, tacrolimus, or other causative immunosuppressant if applicable

Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis - Respirat.pe_findings: Progressive dyspnea, weak cough, inability to clear secretions, orthopnea, morning headaches (CO2 retention), mixed UMN/LMN signs, fasciculations, bulbar weakness

NIF/VC Assessment.rationale: FVC <50% predicted or NIF <-40 → discuss BiPAP; <25% → discuss tracheostomy

BiPAP Initiation.rationale: Non-invasive ventilation improves survival and quality of life

Advance Directive Discussion.rationale: CRITICAL: establish goals of care regarding intubation before crisis

Secretion Management.rationale: Cough assist device, glycopyrrolate for secretions, suction PRN

Parkinson Disease - Acute Akinetic Crisi.pe_findings: Severe rigidity, hyperthermia, autonomic instability, dysphagia/aspiration risk, rhabdomyolysis; triggered by sudden withdrawal or reduction of dopaminergic medications; resembles NMS

Resume Dopaminergic Meds.rationale: NG tube administration of levodopa/carbidopa if unable to swallow

IV Fluids.rationale: Aggressive NS to prevent rhabdomyolysis-induced AKI

Active Cooling.rationale: If hyperthermic; same approach as NMS

Dantrolene.rationale: 1-2.5mg/kg IV if severe rigidity and hyperthermia unresponsive to dopaminergic therapy

Upper GI Bleed - Variceal.pe_findings: Hematemesis (bright red blood or coffee-ground), melena, signs of chronic liver disease (spider angiomata, ascites, jaundice, palmar erythema), splenomegaly, caput medusae

Octreotide.rationale: 50mcg IV bolus then 50mcg/hr drip; reduces splanchnic blood flow

IV PPI.rationale: Pantoprazole 80mg bolus though primarily for non-variceal; start empirically

Emergent EGD.rationale: Band ligation within 12h; definitive treatment for variceal bleed

Ceftriaxone.rationale: 1g IV q24h x 7 days; antibiotic prophylaxis reduces mortality in cirrhotics

pRBC Transfusion.rationale: Target Hgb >7; over-transfusion worsens portal HTN

Upper GI Bleed - Peptic Ulcer.pe_findings: Hematemesis or coffee-ground emesis, melena, epigastric tenderness, possible NSAID/aspirin use, H. pylori history; Rockall/Glasgow-Blatchford scores guide urgency

IV PPI.rationale: Pantoprazole 80mg bolus then 8mg/hr drip; reduces rebleeding

EGD.rationale: Within 24h; epinephrine injection + thermal/clip for active bleeding

H. pylori Testing.rationale: Stool antigen or biopsy at EGD; eradication dramatically reduces recurrence

NSAID Use.risk: increased ulcer size and bleeding risk

Lower GI Bleed - Diverticular.pe_findings: Painless hematochezia (bright red blood per rectum), usually large-volume; typically right-sided diverticula in elderly; 80% stop spontaneously

Colonoscopy.rationale: After bowel prep if hemodynamically stable; identifies source in 70-80%

CTA Abdomen.rationale: If active brisk bleeding; identifies extravasation for IR embolization

Interventional Radiology.rationale: Angiographic embolization if active bleeding identified on CTA

Resuscitation.rationale: IV crystalloid and pRBC as needed; majority stop spontaneously

Acute Pancreatitis - Mild.pe_findings: Severe epigastric pain radiating to back, nausea/vomiting, epigastric tenderness without peritoneal signs, mild distension; Ranson criteria, BISAP score for prognostication

Aggressive IV Fluids.rationale: LR at 5-10mL/kg/hr initial; goal-directed; reduces necrosis risk

Pain Management.rationale: IV hydromorphone or fentanyl; meperidine no longer preferred

NPO Initially.rationale: Start clear liquid diet when pain improving; early feeding improves outcomes

RUQ Ultrasound.rationale: Evaluate for gallstones as etiology; cholecystectomy during same admission if gallstone pancreatitis

Acute Pancreatitis - Severe/Necrotizing.pe_findings: Severe epigastric pain, peritoneal signs, Cullen sign (periumbilical ecchymosis), Grey Turner sign (flank ecchymosis), SIRS/shock, third-spacing with massive fluid losses

CT Abdomen with Contrast.rationale: At 72h to assess necrosis extent; not needed initially

ICU Admission.rationale: Organ failure defines severe pancreatitis per revised Atlanta

Antibiotics.rationale: Only if infected necrosis suspected (fever, gas in collection); imipenem

Nutrition.rationale: Enteral via NJ tube preferred over TPN if unable to eat >5-7 days

Acute Cholecystitis.pe_findings: RUQ pain radiating to right scapula, positive Murphy sign, RUQ guarding, mild jaundice possible; fever suggests complicated (empyema, perforation, gangrenous)

RUQ Ultrasound.rationale: Gallstones + wall thickening >3mm + pericholecystic fluid + sonographic Murphy sign

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Piperacillin-tazobactam or ceftriaxone + metronidazole

Surgical Consult.rationale: Early cholecystectomy within 72h; better outcomes than delayed

HIDA Scan.rationale: If US equivocal; sensitivity >95% for acute cholecystitis

Acute Cholangitis.pe_findings: Charcot triad: RUQ pain, fever, jaundice (50-70%); Reynolds pentad adds AMS and hypotension (septic cholangitis); biliary colic history

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g q6h; cover gram-neg rods and anaerobes

Biliary Decompression.rationale: ERCP within 24-48h; emergent within 12h if septic/Reynolds pentad

Blood Cultures.rationale: Before antibiotics; E. coli, Klebsiella, Enterococcus most common

Acute Appendicitis.pe_findings: Periumbilical pain migrating to RLQ (McBurney point), anorexia, nausea, positive Rovsing/psoas/obturator signs, low-grade fever; guarding and rebound suggest perforation

CT Abdomen/Pelvis.rationale: With IV contrast; >95% sensitivity; dilated appendix >6mm, periappendiceal fat stranding

Surgical Consult.rationale: Laparoscopic appendectomy; <24h if uncomplicated

NPO.rationale: Hold oral intake pending surgical evaluation

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Cefoxitin or ertapenem if perforation suspected; preop prophylaxis for uncomplicated

Perforated Appendicitis with Abscess.pe_findings: RLQ pain (may be diffuse if free perforation), high fever, tachycardia, diffuse peritonitis or palpable RLQ mass, toxic appearance

CT Abdomen/Pelvis.rationale: Perforated appendix with abscess, free fluid, or phlegmon

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Broad-spectrum: piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem

IR Drainage.rationale: Percutaneous drainage if abscess >3cm; antibiotics + interval appendectomy in 6-8 weeks

Surgical Consult.rationale: Emergent OR if diffuse peritonitis or failed non-operative management

Small Bowel Obstruction.pe_findings: Colicky abdominal pain, vomiting (bilious→ feculent in distal SBO), abdominal distension, high-pitched tinkling bowel sounds, prior surgical history (adhesions #1 cause)

CT Abdomen/Pelvis.rationale: Transition point, small bowel dilation >3cm, decompressed distal bowel

NG Tube.rationale: Salem sump to low intermittent suction; decompression and vomiting relief

IV Fluid Resuscitation.rationale: Replace large third-space losses; monitor UOP

Surgical Consult.rationale: Operative if complete obstruction, strangulation (fever, peritonitis, elevated lactate), or failure to resolve in 48-72h

Large Bowel Obstruction.pe_findings: Progressive abdominal distension, obstipation (no gas or stool), crampy pain, vomiting (late finding), tympanic abdomen; colon cancer most common cause

CT Abdomen/Pelvis.rationale: Dilated colon proximal to transition point; evaluate for cecal diameter >12cm (perforation risk)

Surgical Consult.rationale: Emergent if cecal dilation >12cm, perforation, or peritonitis

Rectal Tube/Sigmoidoscopy.rationale: For sigmoid volvulus decompression if no peritonitis

Sigmoid Volvulus.pe_findings: Elderly, institutionalized, psychiatric patients on anticholinergics; massive abdominal distension, obstipation, 'coffee bean sign' on XR, omega loop sign on CT

Abdominal XR.rationale: Classic coffee-bean / bent inner-tube sign; ahaustral dilated sigmoid

Sigmoidoscopy.rationale: Endoscopic detorsion first-line if no peritonitis; successful in 70-80%

Surgical Consult.rationale: Sigmoid colectomy for recurrence, failed detorsion, or gangrenous bowel

Cecal Volvulus.pe_findings: Right-sided abdominal distension and pain, younger than sigmoid volvulus (30-60yrs), dilated cecum displaced to LUQ on imaging; endoscopic detorsion usually NOT effective

CT Abdomen.rationale: Whirl sign, dilated cecum >10cm, ectopic cecum

Surgical Consult.rationale: Right hemicolectomy is definitive treatment; cecopexy if viable bowel

Mesenteric Ischemia - Acute.pe_findings: PAIN OUT OF PROPORTION TO EXAM (classic); severe periumbilical pain, bloody diarrhea (late), metabolic acidosis, AF history (SMA embolism most common); gut feeling of impending doom

CTA Abdomen.rationale: EMERGENT; filling defect in SMA/SMV; bowel wall non-enhancement, pneumatosis

Surgical Consult.rationale: Emergent laparotomy for peritonitis or bowel necrosis

Heparin Drip.rationale: Anticoagulation for thrombotic or embolic causes

Broad-Spectrum Antibiotics.rationale: Bacterial translocation through ischemic gut wall

Atrial Fibrillation.risk: SMA embolism 50% of acute mesenteric ischemia

Mesenteric Ischemia - Chronic.pe_findings: Postprandial pain (intestinal angina) 15-30min after eating, food fear, weight loss, PVD history; abdominal bruit; 2 of 3 mesenteric vessels must be stenosed for symptoms

CTA Abdomen.rationale: Mesenteric artery stenosis/occlusion; collateral vessels may be present

Nutrition.rationale: Small frequent meals; nutritional optimization before intervention

Ischemic Colitis.pe_findings: Crampy left-sided abdominal pain followed by bloody diarrhea within 24h; splenic flexure and rectosigmoid junction (watershed areas) most commonly affected

CT Abdomen.rationale: Bowel wall thickening in watershed distribution; thumbprinting

Colonoscopy.rationale: Within 48h; mucosal edema, hemorrhage, ulceration in segmental pattern

Supportive Care.rationale: Bowel rest, IV fluids, serial abdominal exams; 80% resolve without surgery

Surgical Consult.rationale: For peritonitis, pneumatosis, portal venous gas, or perforation

Acute Liver Failure.pe_findings: Coagulopathy (INR ≥1.5) + encephalopathy in patient WITHOUT pre-existing liver disease; jaundice, RUQ tenderness, cerebral edema, signs of coagulopathy, asterixis early

N-Acetylcysteine.rationale: 21-hour IV protocol; improves survival even in non-acetaminophen ALF

Liver Transplant Evaluation.rationale: Apply King's College Criteria; list if criteria met

Glucose Monitoring.rationale: Q1h; D10 drip for hypoglycemia; impaired gluconeogenesis

Lactulose.rationale: For encephalopathy; although controversial in ALF due to ileus risk

Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis.pe_findings: Fever, abdominal pain, worsening ascites, encephalopathy in cirrhotic patient; may be subtle – always suspect in decompensating cirrhotic

Diagnostic Paracentesis.rationale: PMN >250/uL is diagnostic; do NOT delay for coagulopathy

Cefotaxime.rationale: 2g IV q8h x 5 days; or ceftriaxone 2g IV q24h

IV Albumin.rationale: 1.5g/kg day 1 + 1g/kg day 3; reduces mortality and hepatorenal syndrome

Ascitic Fluid Culture.rationale: Inoculate blood culture bottles at bedside; improves yield

Esophageal Perforation (Boerhaave).pe_findings: Mackler triad: vomiting then chest pain then subcutaneous emphysema; left-sided chest pain after forceful vomiting/retching; Hamman crunch on auscultation; mediastinitis

CT Chest with Oral Contrast.rationale: Pneumomediastinum, pleural effusion, esophageal leak, mediastinal air

Thoracic Surgery Consult.rationale: Emergent operative repair within 24h dramatically improves survival

Broad-Spectrum Antibiotics.rationale: Cover oral flora and gram-negatives; piperacillin-tazobactam + fluconazole

NPO.rationale: Absolute nothing by mouth; NG tube for decompression

Mallory-Weiss Tear.pe_findings: Hematemesis after forceful vomiting/retching; partial-thickness mucosal tear at GEJ; typically self-limited; alcohol use, bulimia, severe retching as risk factors

EGD.rationale: If continued bleeding; epinephrine injection or endoclip; 90% stop spontaneously

Observation.rationale: Most resolve without intervention; monitor for rebleed

Acute Gastritis/Gastropathy.pe_findings: Epigastric pain, nausea, early satiety; may have hematemesis if erosive; NSAID, alcohol, stress-related mucosal disease in ICU patients

PPI.rationale: Omeprazole 40mg IV BID; acid suppression first-line

H. pylori Testing.rationale: Stool antigen or breath test; treat if positive

Remove Offending Agent.rationale: Stop NSAIDs, alcohol, aspirin if possible

Peptic Ulcer Disease - Perforation.pe_findings: Sudden severe epigastric pain ('knife-stab'), rigid board-like abdomen, rebound tenderness, absent bowel sounds, free air under diaphragm on upright CXR

Surgical Consult.rationale: Emergent laparotomy / laparoscopic repair; Graham patch

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Cover gram-negatives and anaerobes: piperacillin-tazobactam

Acute Diverticulitis - Uncomplicated.pe_findings: LLQ pain (rarely right-sided), fever, localized LLQ tenderness with guarding, elevated WBC; left-sided in Western nations, right-sided in Asians

CT Abdomen/Pelvis.rationale: Pericolonic fat stranding, colonic wall thickening, diverticula; Hinchey classification

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Ciprofloxacin + metronidazole or piperacillin-tazobactam

Bowel Rest.rationale: Clear liquids advancing as tolerated; some evidence supports diet with uncomplicated

Colonoscopy.rationale: 6-8 weeks after resolution to rule out underlying malignancy

Acute Diverticulitis - Complicated (Absc.pe_findings: Severe LLQ pain, diffuse peritonitis if free perforation, palpable mass if abscess, septic appearance, high fever; Hinchey III-IV

CT Abdomen/Pelvis.rationale: Abscess, free air, free fluid; Hinchey staging

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Broad-spectrum: piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem

IR Drainage.rationale: Percutaneous for abscess >3cm; bridge to elective surgery

Surgical Consult.rationale: Emergent Hartmann procedure for Hinchey III/IV (free perforation)

Toxic Megacolon.pe_findings: Severe abdominal distension and pain, fever, tachycardia, bloody diarrhea, altered mental status in IBD flare or C. diff; transverse colon dilation >6cm on XR

Abdominal XR.rationale: Transverse colon >6cm; haustral loss; serial XR q12h

IV Corticosteroids.rationale: Methylprednisolone 60mg/day if IBD-related; contraindicated in C. diff

Surgical Consult.rationale: Subtotal colectomy if no improvement in 24-72h, perforation, or hemorrhage

C. difficile Colitis - Fulminant.pe_findings: Profuse watery diarrhea (may paradoxically decrease with ileus), abdominal distension, severe tenderness, WBC >15 or rising Cr; recent antibiotics/hospitalization

PO Vancomycin.rationale: 500mg PO/NG q6h; first-line for fulminant C. diff

IV Metronidazole.rationale: 500mg IV q8h; adjunctive for fulminant disease

Vancomycin Retention Enema.rationale: 500mg in 100mL NS PR q6h if ileus (poor oral delivery to colon)

Surgical Consult.rationale: Subtotal colectomy for refractory disease, toxic megacolon, perforation, or WBC >20 + lactate >5

CT Abdomen.rationale: Colonic wall thickening, pericolonic stranding, ascites; evaluate for megacolon

Acute Hepatitis - Viral.pe_findings: Jaundice, RUQ pain, nausea/anorexia, malaise, dark urine, clay-colored stools, hepatomegaly, fever in prodrome; HAV fecal-oral, HBV/HCV blood-borne

Hepatitis Panel.rationale: HAV IgM, HBsAg, HBcAb IgM, HCV Ab + RNA

PT/INR Monitoring.rationale: Rising INR suggests impending liver failure → transfer to transplant center

Achalasia.pe_findings: Progressive dysphagia to BOTH solids AND liquids (mechanical is solids first), regurgitation of undigested food, chest pain, weight loss, aspiration pneumonia risk

Barium Swallow.rationale: Bird's beak narrowing at GEJ, dilated esophageal body

Esophageal Manometry.rationale: Gold standard; aperistalsis + failure of LES relaxation

Heller Myotomy or POEM.rationale: Definitive surgical treatment; Botox injection as bridge

Ogilvie Syndrome (Acute Colonic Pseudo-o.pe_findings: Massive colonic distension WITHOUT mechanical obstruction; post-operative, critically ill, or elderly patients; cecal dilation >12cm = perforation risk

Abdominal XR/CT.rationale: Diffuse colonic dilation; CT to exclude mechanical obstruction

Neostigmine.rationale: 2mg IV over 3-5min with cardiac monitoring and atropine at bedside; 90% response rate

Colonoscopic Decompression.rationale: If neostigmine fails; decompression tube placement

Surgical Consult.rationale: Cecostomy tube or subtotal colectomy if refractory/perforated

Hepatorenal Syndrome.pe_findings: Progressive oliguria in decompensating cirrhotic with refractory ascites; diagnosis of exclusion; bland urine sediment, UNa <10, normal kidney on US

Octreotide + Midodrine.rationale: Splanchnic vasoconstriction + increase MAP; or norepinephrine drip in ICU

Terlipressin.rationale: Vasopressin analog; approved for HRS in many countries; improves renal function

Liver Transplant Evaluation.rationale: Definitive treatment; prognoses very poor without transplant

GI Foreign Body Ingestion.pe_findings: Dysphagia, drooling, chest/abdominal pain, refusal to eat (pediatric); button battery and magnet ingestion are emergencies; sharp objects risk perforation

XR Neck/Chest/Abdomen.rationale: Identify location, type, number of objects

Emergent EGD.rationale: Required for: button battery in esophagus, sharp objects, food impaction >24h, magnets

Button Battery Protocol.rationale: Esophageal button battery = immediate removal; can cause perforation/fistula in 2h; honey q10min while awaiting removal

Observation.rationale: Blunt objects past pylorus → serial XR; most pass spontaneously in 4-6 days

Acute Gastroenteritis - Severe Dehydrati.pe_findings: Profuse watery diarrhea and/or vomiting, dry mucous membranes, decreased skin turgor, orthostasis, sunken eyes, delayed capillary refill, confusion in severe cases

IV Fluid Resuscitation.rationale: NS or LR bolus 20mL/kg; repeat as needed for perfusion

Electrolyte Correction.rationale: Replace K, Mg, correct metabolic acidosis

Stool Studies.rationale: Culture, C. diff, O&P, viral panel if immunocompromised

Antiemetics.rationale: Ondansetron 4mg IV to enable oral rehydration

Sepsis - qSOFA Positive.pe_findings: Altered mental status, tachypnea >=22, SBP <=100; suspected infection; may have localizing signs of source (pneumonia, UTI, cellulitis, intra-abdominal)

Blood Cultures.rationale: Before antibiotics; 2 sets from different sites

Broad-Spectrum Antibiotics.rationale: Within 1h of recognition per Surviving Sepsis Campaign; each hour delay increases mortality 7.6%

Lactate.rationale: If >2 repeat in 2-4h to guide resuscitation; clearance >10%/hr is goal

Septic Shock.pe_findings: Sepsis + vasopressor requirement to maintain MAP >=65 AND lactate >2 despite adequate fluid resuscitation; warm shock (early: warm, flushed) → cold shock (late: cool, mottled)

Broad-Spectrum Antibiotics.rationale: WITHIN 1 HOUR; empiric based on suspected source

Norepinephrine.rationale: First-line vasopressor; start if MAP <65 despite fluids; target MAP ≥65

Vasopressin.rationale: Add at 0.03 U/min as second vasopressor; norepi-sparing

Central Venous Access.rationale: CVC for vasopressor administration; ScvO2 monitoring

Endocarditis - Acute.pe_findings: Fever, new or changing murmur, Janeway lesions (painless palms/soles), Osler nodes (painful fingertips), Roth spots (retinal hemorrhages), splinter hemorrhages, splenomegaly

Blood Cultures.rationale: 3 sets from different sites before antibiotics; continuous bacteremia hallmark

TTE/TEE.rationale: TEE preferred (>90% sensitivity for vegetations); TTE first if available immediately

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Empiric: vancomycin + cefepime; MSSA: nafcillin; 4-6 week course per AHA

Surgical Consult.rationale: Urgent for HF from valve destruction, uncontrolled infection, large vegetation (>10mm), embolic events

Endocarditis - Subacute.pe_findings: Insidious fever, weight loss, night sweats, new murmur; peripheral stigmata more common than acute; Viridans strep typical; preexisting valve disease

Blood Cultures x3.rationale: 3 sets from different sites over 24h before antibiotics if stable

TTE then TEE.rationale: Modified Duke criteria; vegetations, abscess, new regurgitation

IV Antibiotics.rationale: May wait 24h for culture results if clinically stable; targeted therapy preferred

Embolic Workup.rationale: Brain MRI, abdominal CT for splenic abscess, retinal exam

Pyelonephritis - Complicated.pe_findings: Fever, flank pain, CVA tenderness, rigors; complicated: obstruction, abscess, pregnancy, male, immunocompromised, catheter-associated; may progress to urosepsis

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Ceftriaxone 2g IV or fluoroquinolone; add vancomycin if MRSA risk

Renal Ultrasound/CT.rationale: Evaluate for hydronephrosis, perinephric abscess, obstructing stone

Blood Cultures.rationale: If septic; E. coli most common organism

Urology Consult.rationale: If obstructing stone with infection → emergent decompression (stent or nephrostomy)

Peritonsillar Abscess.pe_findings: Severe sore throat (usually unilateral), trismus, hot potato voice, uvular deviation, peritonsillar bulging, drooling, fever; most common deep space neck infection

Needle Aspiration or I&D.rationale: Needle aspiration first; I&D if fails; both diagnostic and therapeutic

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Ampicillin-sulbactam or clindamycin; cover GAS and oropharyngeal anaerobes

IV Dexamethasone.rationale: Single dose reduces pain and trismus; speeds recovery

Airway Assessment.rationale: Rare airway compromise but must assess; have difficult airway equipment ready

Ludwig Angina.pe_findings: Submandibular space infection; floor of mouth elevation, tongue protrusion, woody induration of submandibular area, drooling, stridor; AIRWAY EMERGENCY; dental source typical

Airway Management.rationale: PRIMARY CONCERN; fiberoptic intubation or surgical airway may be needed; DO NOT attempt blind intubation

CT Neck with Contrast.rationale: Evaluate extent, drainable collection, mediastinal extension

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Ampicillin-sulbactam + metronidazole; or penicillin + metronidazole + ceftriaxone

Surgical Consult.rationale: Surgical drainage if abscess; may need tooth extraction

Dexamethasone.rationale: IV steroids to reduce edema and improve airway

Retropharyngeal Abscess.pe_findings: Severe neck pain/stiffness (mimics meningitis), dysphagia, drooling, muffled voice, neck hyperextension/torticollis; children <5y most common; adult variant from spinal procedures or foreign body

Lateral Neck XR.rationale: Prevertebral soft tissue widening >7mm at C2 or >14mm at C6 (children)

CT Neck with Contrast.rationale: Ring-enhancing collection in retropharyngeal space; evaluate mediastinal extension

Airway Management.rationale: Highest priority; stridor or respiratory distress → secure airway

Surgical Drainage.rationale: Transoral or external approach depending on extent; ENT consult

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Ampicillin-sulbactam or clindamycin; cover GAS, oral anaerobes, Staph

Cellulitis - Severe / Purulent.pe_findings: Expanding erythema with warmth, tenderness, induration; purulent = abscess/MRSA likely; red streaking (lymphangitis), regional lymphadenopathy; demarcate borders with marking pen

I&D if Abscess.rationale: Incision and drainage is MOST IMPORTANT intervention for purulent cellulitis

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Purulent: vancomycin or daptomycin (MRSA coverage); non-purulent: cefazolin or ceftriaxone (beta-hemolytic strep)

Mark Borders.rationale: Draw on skin with marker to track progression vs regression

Ultrasound.rationale: Bedside US detects abscess with 90% sensitivity; guides I&D

Osteomyelitis - Acute.pe_findings: Localized bone pain, fever, swelling, erythema over bone; pediatric: long bone metaphysis (hematogenous); adult: vertebral body or contiguous spread from wound/surgery; diabetic foot

MRI.rationale: Most sensitive (90%) and specific (82%) imaging modality; marrow edema, periosteal reaction

Blood Cultures.rationale: Positive in 50% of hematogenous osteomyelitis; send before antibiotics

Bone Biopsy/Culture.rationale: Gold standard for organism identification; guides targeted therapy

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Empiric: vancomycin (MRSA) + ceftriaxone; total 4-6 weeks IV therapy

Surgical Consult.rationale: Debridement if abscess, sequestrum, or failed medical therapy

Diabetes.risk: diabetic foot osteomyelitis; probe-to-bone test 66% PPV

Septic Arthritis.pe_findings: Hot, swollen, erythematous joint with SEVERE pain on passive ROM; monoarticular (usually knee); inability to bear weight; Staph aureus most common; gonococcal in young sexually active

Arthrocentesis.rationale: MANDATORY; synovial WBC >50K highly suggestive; gram stain, culture, crystal analysis to exclude gout

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Vancomycin (MRSA coverage) + ceftriaxone; narrow when cultures return

Orthopedic Consult.rationale: Surgical drainage/lavage especially for hip or prosthetic joint; serial aspirations for other joints

Blood Cultures.rationale: Often positive; particularly in non-gonococcal septic arthritis

Tetanus.pe_findings: Trismus (lockjaw), risus sardonicus (sardonic smile), opisthotonus, generalized muscle rigidity and spasms, autonomic instability; wound may be minor/unrecognized; unvaccinated

Tetanus Immune Globulin.rationale: TIG 500 IU IM; neutralizes unbound toxin

Wound Debridement.rationale: Remove necrotic tissue; reduce toxin production

Metronidazole.rationale: 500mg IV q6h x 10 days; kills C. tetani (alternative: penicillin G)

Benzodiazepines.rationale: Diazepam 10mg IV q1h PRN for spasm control; may need continuous infusion

Intubation Prep.rationale: Laryngospasm/respiratory failure may require RSI; avoid succinylcholine

Rabies - Symptomatic.pe_findings: Prodrome: paresthesias at bite site, fever, malaise → Furious (80%): hydrophobia, aerophobia, agitation, hallucinations, autonomic instability → Paralytic (20%): ascending paralysis like GBS

Milwaukee Protocol.rationale: Therapeutic coma attempt; <20% survival even with treatment; nearly 100% fatal once symptomatic

Post-Exposure Prophylaxis for Contacts.rationale: Any healthcare worker with mucous membrane/wound exposure to saliva

Malaria - Severe (P. falciparum).pe_findings: High fever with rigors (periodicity may be absent in falciparum), AMS/cerebral malaria, severe anemia, ARDS, hypoglycemia, metabolic acidosis, DIC; travel to endemic area

IV Artesunate.rationale: First-line for severe malaria (2.4mg/kg IV at 0, 12, 24h then daily); superior to quinine

Exchange Transfusion.rationale: Consider if parasitemia >10% or cerebral malaria with high burden

Glucose Monitoring.rationale: Q4h; hypoglycemia from parasite consumption and quinine effect

Supportive Care.rationale: Fluid resuscitation (careful - ARDS risk), seizure management, transfusion for Hgb <7

Toxic Shock Syndrome - Staphylococcal.pe_findings: Fever ≥102°F, diffuse macular rash (sunburn-like), desquamation 1-2 weeks later (especially palms/soles), hypotension, multiorgan involvement (≥3 organ systems); menstrual or wound associated

Remove Foreign Body.rationale: Remove tampon, wound packing, or retained foreign body immediately

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Vancomycin + clindamycin (toxin suppression is key; clindamycin inhibits toxin production even if resistant)

IVIG.rationale: 2g/kg single dose; neutralizes TSST-1 and other superantigens; consider for refractory cases

Toxic Shock Syndrome - Streptococcal.pe_findings: Severe pain at infection site (often necrotizing fasciitis), rapidly progressive shock, multiorgan failure; NO rash (vs staphylococcal); GAS bacteremia in 60%

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Penicillin G + clindamycin; clindamycin Eagle effect — more effective than penicillin alone at high inoculum

Surgical Debridement.rationale: Emergent; often necrotizing fasciitis; wide debridement critical

ICU Care.rationale: Vasopressors, mechanical ventilation, CRRT for renal failure often required

Herpes Simplex Encephalitis.pe_findings: Fever, headache, altered mental status, bizarre behavior, olfactory/gustatory hallucinations, temporal lobe seizures, focal neurological deficits; HSV-1 in adults, HSV-2 in neonates

IV Acyclovir.rationale: 10mg/kg IV q8h x 14-21 days; START EMPIRICALLY — do NOT wait for HSV PCR results; reduces mortality from 70% to 20%

LP with HSV PCR.rationale: CSF HSV PCR 96% sensitive after 72h; may be negative early — repeat in 3-7 days if high suspicion

MRI Brain.rationale: Temporal lobe edema/hemorrhage; most sensitive imaging

EEG.rationale: Periodic lateralized epileptiform discharges (PLEDs) from temporal lobe

Tuberculosis - Active Pulmonary.pe_findings: Chronic cough >2 weeks, hemoptysis, night sweats, weight loss, fever; upper lobe cavitation on CXR; immigrants, homeless, incarcerated, HIV, healthcare workers

Airborne Isolation.rationale: IMMEDIATE N95 respirator + negative pressure room; highest priority

AFB Smear and Culture.rationale: 3 sputum specimens (8h apart); nucleic acid amplification test (NAAT) for rapid confirmation

CXR.rationale: Upper lobe infiltrates, cavitation, lymphadenopathy; miliary pattern if disseminated

RIPE Therapy.rationale: Rifampin + Isoniazid + Pyrazinamide + Ethambutol x 2 months, then RH x 4 months

HIV.risk: 50-fold increased risk; atypical CXR in advanced HIV

Tuberculosis - Miliary/Disseminated.pe_findings: Fever, weight loss, hepatosplenomegaly, pancytopenia, diffuse miliary pattern on CXR (1-3mm nodules throughout both lungs), choroidal tubercles on fundoscopy; immunocompromised host

RIPE Therapy.rationale: Start empirically if miliary pattern; often before culture confirmation

Blood/Bone Marrow Culture.rationale: Higher yield than sputum in miliary TB

Corticosteroids.rationale: Dexamethasone if TB meningitis suspected; reduces mortality

HIV Testing.rationale: Miliary TB frequently presents in advanced HIV; CD4 count critical

Influenza - Severe/Complicated.pe_findings: High fever, myalgias, cough, dyspnea, tachypnea; complications: primary viral pneumonia, secondary bacterial pneumonia, myocarditis, rhabdomyolysis, encephalitis; risk groups: elderly, pregnant, immunocompromised

Oseltamivir.rationale: 75mg BID x 5 days; most benefit within 48h of symptoms but give even if late in severe disease

Respiratory Support.rationale: HFNC, BiPAP, or intubation as needed for respiratory failure

Bacterial Superinfection Evaluation.rationale: Blood/sputum cultures; procalcitonin trending; add antibiotics if secondary pneumonia suspected

Droplet + Contact Precautions.rationale: Standard infection prevention; airborne for aerosol-generating procedures

COVID-19 - Severe Pneumonia.pe_findings: Hypoxemia (often 'happy hypoxia' initially), dyspnea, bilateral ground-glass opacities, prone positioning helps, cytokine storm features; hypercoagulable; ARDS progression

Dexamethasone.rationale: 6mg/day x 10 days; benefit ONLY if requiring O2 (RECOVERY trial); reduces mortality

Prone Positioning.rationale: 16h/day prone; improves V/Q matching and oxygenation; reduces intubation

Anticoagulation.rationale: Prophylactic enoxaparin for non-ICU; therapeutic for ICU patients (per ATTACC/REMAP-CAP)

Tocilizumab.rationale: If CRP >75 and on O2 within 24h of ICU admission; IL-6 receptor blocker

HFNC/NIV.rationale: High-flow nasal cannula superior to standard O2 for avoiding intubation

Fournier Gangrene.pe_findings: Necrotizing fasciitis of perineum/genitalia; severe perineal/scrotal pain, swelling, crepitus, rapid progression, foul smell, skin necrosis; diabetic and immunocompromised at risk

Emergent Surgical Debridement.rationale: MOST CRITICAL intervention; wide radical debridement; mortality directly correlates with time to OR

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam + clindamycin; polymicrobial infection

ICU Admission.rationale: High mortality (20-40%); frequent return to OR for re-debridement

CT Pelvis.rationale: Gas tracking, extent of disease; but NEVER delay OR for imaging if clinical diagnosis clear

Lyme Disease - Acute Disseminated.pe_findings: Multiple erythema migrans lesions, facial nerve palsy (bilateral pathognomonic), AV block (PR prolongation, complete heart block), meningitis, migratory arthralgias; tick exposure history

Lyme Serology.rationale: Two-tier testing: EIA/IFA then Western blot if positive; may be negative in first 2 weeks

ECG.rationale: PR prolongation or high-degree AV block (Lyme carditis); may need temporary pacer

LP.rationale: If meningitis suspected; Lyme CSF antibody index

Doxycycline.rationale: 100mg BID x 21-28 days for disseminated; IV ceftriaxone for meningitis or 3rd degree AVB

Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever.pe_findings: Fever, headache, myalgia then classic petechial rash starting ankles/wrists → spreading centrIPETally (palms & soles involved); tick exposure (Dermacentor); 20% mortality if untreated

Doxycycline.rationale: 100mg BID for ALL ages including children; START immediately on clinical suspicion — do NOT wait for serology

Supportive Care.rationale: Aggressive fluid resuscitation for capillary leak; platelets for active bleeding only

Serology.rationale: Antibody rise takes 7-10 days; treatment must begin empirically; PCR available

Dengue - Severe (Dengue Hemorrhagic Feve.pe_findings: Fever, severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, mucosal bleeding, rapid hematocrit rise (hemoconcentration), pleural effusions, ascites; WARNING SIGNS present in defervescence phase

IV Crystalloid.rationale: 5-10mL/kg/hr LR; reduce as hematocrit falls; AVOID over-resuscitation (effusions)

Serial Hematocrit.rationale: Q4-6h; rising Hct = ongoing plasma leak; falling Hct may = hemorrhage

Platelet Monitoring.rationale: Transfuse only for active bleeding or <10K; prophylactic transfusion NOT recommended

Avoid NSAIDs/Aspirin.rationale: Increased bleeding risk; use acetaminophen only for fever

Pneumonia - Healthcare-Associated/Ventil.pe_findings: New infiltrate + purulent secretions, fever or leukocytosis in hospitalized/ventilated patient ≥48h; MDR risk: Pseudomonas, MRSA, Acinetobacter, ESBL-producing organisms

Respiratory Culture.rationale: Quantitative tracheal aspirate or BAL; before new antibiotics

Broad-Spectrum Antibiotics.rationale: Anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam + vancomycin or linezolid (MRSA); double gram-neg if MDR risk

De-escalation.rationale: Narrow based on culture + sensitivities within 48-72h; shorter courses (7 days) if improving

CXR.rationale: New or progressive infiltrate; daily in ventilated patients

Catheter-Related Bloodstream Infection.pe_findings: Fever, rigors, exit site erythema/purulence, bacteremia with no other identified source in patient with central line; differential time to positivity (CVC blood+ ≥2h before peripheral)

Blood Cultures.rationale: Paired: one from CVC + one peripheral; differential time to positivity

Remove CVC.rationale: Remove line for S. aureus, Candida, or clinical deterioration; exchange over wire for some gram-negatives

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Vancomycin empirically; add gram-negative coverage if septic; duration based on organism

TTE/TEE.rationale: For S. aureus bacteremia — always rule out endocarditis; 25% have vegetation

Hyperthyroidism - Graves Disease.pe_findings: Diffuse goiter, exophthalmos, lid lag, pretibial myxedema, fine tremor, hyperreflexia, warm moist skin, wide pulse pressure, atrial fibrillation

Beta-Blocker.rationale: Propranolol 40-80mg q6h for symptom control; also blocks T4→T3 conversion

Methimazole.rationale: Start 10-40mg/day; titrate based on free T4/T3; monitor CBC for agranulocytosis

Hypothyroidism - Myxedema Coma.pe_findings: Hypothermia, altered mental status, bradycardia, hypotension, hypoventilation, non-pitting edema (myxedema), periorbital puffiness, delayed DTR relaxation phase, macroglossia

IV Levothyroxine.rationale: 200-400mcg IV loading dose; then 50-100mcg/day IV; oral absorption unreliable

IV Hydrocortisone.rationale: 100mg IV q8h BEFORE thyroid hormone; concomitant adrenal insufficiency may precipitate crisis

Passive Rewarming.rationale: Warm blankets; avoid active rewarming (vasodilation → cardiovascular collapse)

Ventilatory Support.rationale: Intubate for CO2 narcosis; impaired respiratory drive

Pheochromocytoma Crisis.pe_findings: Episodic TRIAD: headache + sweating + palpitations; severe paroxysmal hypertension, pallor (not flushing), anxiety, tremor, chest/abdominal pain; triggered by surgery, anesthesia, medications

IV Phentolamine.rationale: 5mg IV bolus q5min for acute crisis; alpha-blocker first-line for acute management

NEVER Beta-Blocker First.rationale: CRITICAL: beta-blockade BEFORE alpha-blockade → unopposed alpha-stimulation → hypertensive crisis

CT/MRI Abdomen.rationale: After stabilization; locate tumor (90% adrenal); CT with contrast

Phenoxybenzamine.rationale: Long-acting oral alpha-blocker for preoperative preparation x 10-14 days before surgery

Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State.pe_findings: Severe dehydration (often 9-12L deficit), altered mental status (proportional to osmolality), glucose >600, osmolality >320, minimal ketosis; elderly T2DM; infection common precipitant

IV NS 1L/hr.rationale: Aggressive fluid resuscitation: 1-1.5L/hr first 2h; switch to 0.45% NS when Na corrects

Insulin Drip.rationale: 0.1 U/kg/hr BUT only after K >3.3 and adequate volume resuscitation; too-early insulin worsens outcomes

Potassium Replacement.rationale: Replace before insulin if K <3.3; add 20-40 mEq/L to fluids if 3.3-5.3

DVT Prophylaxis.rationale: Hyperviscosity creates high thrombotic risk; enoxaparin when safe

Hypoglycemia - Severe.pe_findings: Whipple triad: symptoms + glucose <70 + resolution with glucose; adrenergic: tremor, diaphoresis, tachycardia, anxiety; neuroglycopenic: confusion, seizure, coma, focal deficits mimicking stroke

IV Dextrose.rationale: D50W 25-50mL IV push (12.5-25g glucose); repeat in 10-15min if no response; D10 gtt maintenance

Glucagon.rationale: 1mg IM if no IV access; less effective in alcoholics/liver disease (depleted glycogen)

Recheck Glucose.rationale: Q15min until >100; then q1h x 4h; sulfonylurea hypoglycemia requires prolonged monitoring/octreotide

Identify Cause.rationale: Insulin/sulfonylurea dose? Missed meal? Adrenal insufficiency? Insulinoma? Factitious?

Hyperkalemia - Critical.pe_findings: Muscle weakness (ascending), paresthesias, palpitations, ECG changes: peaked T waves → loss of P waves → widened QRS → sine wave → VF/asystole; renal failure most common cause

IV Calcium.rationale: Calcium gluconate 10mL of 10% over 2-3min; membrane stabilizer; repeat if persistent ECG changes

Insulin + Glucose.rationale: 10 units regular insulin IV + D50 25g; shifts K intracellularly; onset 15-30min

Albuterol.rationale: 10-20mg nebulized; shifts K intracellularly; additive with insulin

Sodium Bicarbonate.rationale: 50mEq IV if metabolic acidosis (pH <7.2); modest K-lowering effect

Kayexalate or Patiromer.rationale: Sodium polystyrene or patiromer for GI K removal; slower onset

Emergent Dialysis.rationale: If refractory, K >7, or severe renal failure; definitive treatment

Hypokalemia - Severe.pe_findings: Muscle weakness (may progress to paralysis), cramps, rhabdomyolysis, constipation/ileus, ECG: U waves, flattened T waves, ST depression, prolonged QT → torsades; hypomagnesemia often coexists

IV KCl Replacement.rationale: 10-20 mEq/hr via central line (max 40 mEq/hr in critical); peripheral max 10 mEq/hr; cardiac monitoring REQUIRED

Magnesium Repletion.rationale: 2g MgSO4 IV; K cannot be corrected without adequate Mg

Telemetry.rationale: Continuous cardiac monitoring for dysrhythmias; QT prolongation → torsades risk

Recheck K q2h.rationale: Frequent monitoring during aggressive repletion; large deficit (200-400 mEq total body deficit for each 1 mEq/L drop)

Hyponatremia - Severe Symptomatic.pe_findings: Headache, nausea, AMS, seizures, coma when Na <120; causes: SIADH, polydipsia, diuretics, adrenal insufficiency, hypothyroidism; cerebral edema risk

3% Hypertonic Saline.rationale: 100mL IV over 10min if seizure/severe symptoms; may repeat x2; aim Na increase 4-6 mEq/L in first 6h

Na Correction Rate.rationale: MAX 8 mEq/L in 24h to avoid osmotic demyelination syndrome (central pontine myelinolysis)

Identify Etiology.rationale: Serum/urine osm, urine Na, volume status assessment; SIADH vs cerebral salt wasting vs volume depletion

Q2h Na Monitoring.rationale: Serial sodium levels to ensure safe correction rate

Hypernatremia - Severe.pe_findings: Lethargy, irritability, hyperreflexia, seizures (in rapid-onset), intracranial hemorrhage (brain shrinks → bridging veins tear); elderly, restricted water access, diabetes insipidus

Free Water Replacement.rationale: D5W or 0.45% NS; correct Na no faster than 10-12 mEq/L per 24h to avoid cerebral edema

Calculate Free Water Deficit.rationale: FWD = TBW x [(Na/140) - 1]; replace deficit + ongoing losses over 48-72h

Address Underlying Cause.rationale: DI (central vs nephrogenic): desmopressin trial; osmotic diuresis; inadequate intake

Q4h Na Monitoring.rationale: Serial levels to ensure safe correction rate

Hypercalcemia Crisis.pe_findings: Stones, bones, groans, moans, psychiatric overtones: renal stones, bone pain, abdominal pain, nausea/vomiting, AMS/confusion; shortened QT on ECG; malignancy and hyperparathyroidism account for 90%

IV NS Aggressive.rationale: 200-500mL/hr; restore volume and increase renal Ca excretion

Calcitonin.rationale: 4 IU/kg SC/IM q12h; rapid onset (4-6h) but tachyphylaxis in 48h; bridge therapy

Zoledronic Acid.rationale: 4mg IV over 15min; onset 2-4 days; first-line for malignancy-related hypercalcemia

Furosemide.rationale: 40mg IV ONLY after adequate volume resuscitation; promotes calciuresis; NOT first-line

Dialysis.rationale: For refractory or renal failure patients; low-calcium bath

Lactic Acidosis.pe_findings: Kussmaul respirations, tachycardia, hypotension if severe, altered mental status; Type A (hypoperfusion: shock, cardiac arrest, mesenteric ischemia) vs Type B (metabolic: metformin, HIV meds, malignancy)

Treat Underlying Cause.rationale: MOST IMPORTANT; restore perfusion for Type A; stop offending agent for Type B

IV Fluid Resuscitation.rationale: NS or LR for hypovolemic/distributive shock

Sodium Bicarbonate.rationale: Controversial; consider if pH <7.1 and hemodynamically unstable; 1-2 mEq/kg IV

Serial Lactate.rationale: Q2-4h; clearance >10%/hr is good prognostic sign

Dialysis.rationale: For metformin-associated lactic acidosis; removes metformin and corrects acidosis

Diabetic Foot Infection - Severe.pe_findings: Deep ulcer extending to bone/joint, purulent drainage, surrounding cellulitis >2cm, crepitus (gas gangrene), systemic toxicity; PEDIS or IWGDF classification; polymicrobial; probe-to-bone test

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam; cover MRSA + gram-neg + anaerobes

Wound Assessment.rationale: Depth, probe-to-bone, vascular assessment (ABI, toe pressures)

MRI Foot.rationale: Most sensitive for osteomyelitis; bone biopsy gold standard

Surgical Debridement.rationale: Remove necrotic tissue; may need partial amputation

Hyperglycemic Emergency in Pregnancy.pe_findings: DKA in pregnancy occurs at LOWER glucose levels (euglycemic DKA); faster onset, more dangerous; fetal distress common; risk factors: type 1 DM, new-onset, steroid use, tocolytic use (terbutaline)

Insulin Drip.rationale: 0.1 U/kg/hr; same protocol as non-pregnant DKA

Aggressive IV Fluids.rationale: NS 1L first hour; adequate hydration critical for uteroplacental perfusion

Continuous Fetal Monitoring.rationale: Fetal distress resolves with maternal stabilization; AVOID emergent delivery until maternal pH corrected

K Replacement.rationale: Replace aggressively; larger shifts during treatment

Acute Kidney Injury - Prerenal.pe_findings: Decreased UOP, orthostasis, dry mucous membranes, poor skin turgor; FENa <1%, BUN:Cr >20:1; causes: hypovolemia, heart failure, hepatorenal, NSAIDs, ACE-I

Monitor UOP.rationale: Target >0.5 mL/kg/hr; Foley for accurate measurement

Renal Ultrasound.rationale: Rule out obstruction if no response to fluids

Acute Kidney Injury - Intrinsic (ATN).pe_findings: Oliguria or non-oliguria; muddy brown granular casts on UA; FENa >2%; causes: prolonged ischemia (post-prerenal), nephrotoxins (contrast, aminoglycosides, cisplatin), rhabdomyolysis

Volume Optimization.rationale: Euvolemia — avoid both hypovolemia and overload

Nephrology Consult.rationale: Consider dialysis if refractory electrolyte derangements, volume overload, uremia, acidosis

Acute Kidney Injury - Postrenal (Obstruc.pe_findings: Anuria (complete obstruction) or fluctuating UOP; suprapubic distension (bladder outlet), flank pain (ureteral); BPH, stones, malignancy; bilateral or solitary kidney obstruction required for AKI

Foley Catheter.rationale: Immediate trial; therapeutic for bladder outlet obstruction; monitor for post-obstructive diuresis

Urology Consult.rationale: Nephrostomy tube or ureteral stent for upper tract obstruction

Post-Obstructive Diuresis.rationale: Monitor after relief: may lose 200-500mL/hr; replace 50% of UOP with 0.45% NS

Nephrotic Syndrome.pe_findings: Massive proteinuria (>3.5g/day), hypoalbuminemia, edema (periorbital→dependent→anasarca), hyperlipidemia; hypercoagulable state (renal vein thrombosis); causes: minimal change, membranous, FSGS, diabetic

Renal Biopsy.rationale: Definitive diagnosis in adults; guides treatment

Anticoagulation.rationale: Low threshold for DVT/PE evaluation; prophylactic if severe hypoalbuminemia

Diuretics + Albumin.rationale: IV albumin 25% + furosemide for refractory edema; albumin improves diuretic delivery

ACE-I/ARB.rationale: Reduce proteinuria; first-line for all nephrotic syndrome

Nephritic Syndrome / Rapidly Progressive.pe_findings: Hematuria (RBC casts pathognomonic), proteinuria (sub-nephrotic <3.5g), hypertension, edema, oliguria; rapid Cr rise over days-weeks; causes: anti-GBM, ANCA vasculitis, lupus nephritis, post-infectious, IgA

Urgent Renal Biopsy.rationale: Crescentic GN on biopsy; guides immunosuppression

Pulse Methylprednisolone.rationale: 1g IV daily x 3 days for suspected RPGN while awaiting biopsy

Plasmapheresis.rationale: For anti-GBM disease and ANCA vasculitis with pulmonary hemorrhage

Serologic Workup.rationale: ANCA, anti-GBM, ANA, complements, anti-dsDNA, ASO

Dialysis.rationale: If uremic or refractory to immunosuppression

Renal Colic - Obstructing Stone.pe_findings: Severe colicky flank pain radiating to groin (ureteral), restless/writhing (unlike peritonitis), nausea/vomiting, CVA tenderness, hematuria (85%), ipsilateral testicular or labial pain from referred innervation

CT Abdomen/Pelvis (Non-contrast).rationale: Gold standard; identifies stone, location, size, hydronephrosis; 97% sensitivity

IV Ketorolac.rationale: 30mg IV; first-line analgesic — equivalent to opioids for renal colic with fewer side effects

IV Fluids.rationale: Maintain hydration; do NOT force fluids (no benefit and increases pain)

Tamsulosin.rationale: 0.4mg daily for medical expulsive therapy; stones 5-10mm; speeds passage

Urology Consult.rationale: Stone >10mm, infected hydronephrosis (urosepsis), bilateral obstruction, solitary kidney, refractory pain

Urinary Retention - Acute.pe_findings: Suprapubic pain and distension, inability to void, palpable bladder, agitation; BPH most common cause in males; anticholinergics, opioids, post-surgical in both sexes

Monitor for Post-Obstructive Diuresis.rationale: If >1500mL drained initially; can occur after prolonged retention

Alpha-Blocker.rationale: Tamsulosin 0.4mg daily for BPH-related; trial of void after catheter in 3-7 days

Medication Review.rationale: Stop anticholinergics, antihistamines, opioids if contributing

Testicular Torsion.pe_findings: Sudden severe unilateral scrotal pain, high-riding testis, absent cremasteric reflex, horizontal lie, nausea/vomiting; peak: neonatal and adolescent; 'bell-clapper' deformity predisposes

Manual Detorsion.rationale: Open-book detorsion (medial to lateral); provides immediate relief while awaiting OR

Emergent Urology Consult.rationale: Surgical exploration and orchiopexy within 6h; >6h = increasing testicular loss; >24h = near 100% loss

Doppler Ultrasound.rationale: If diagnosis uncertain; absent/decreased blood flow; but do NOT delay OR for imaging if clinical suspicion high

Bilateral Orchiopexy.rationale: Fix both testes; bell-clapper is bilateral anomaly

Priapism - Ischemic.pe_findings: Painful sustained erection >4h; fully rigid corpora cavernosa, soft glans/corpus spongiosum; dark blood on aspiration (ischemic); sickle cell disease, medications (trazodone, PDE5i overdose)

Corporal Aspiration.rationale: Aspirate dark deoxygenated blood from corpus cavernosum; send ABG of aspirated blood (pO2 <40 confirms ischemic)

Phenylephrine Injection.rationale: 100-500mcg intracavernosal q3-5min (max 1mg); alpha-agonist contracts smooth muscle

Urology Consult.rationale: Emergent; if aspiration + phenylephrine fails → surgical shunt

Treat Underlying Cause.rationale: Sickle cell: hydration + exchange transfusion; stop offending medication

Blunt Abdominal Trauma - Splenic Injury.pe_findings: LUQ pain, left shoulder pain (Kehr sign from diaphragmatic irritation), guarding, distension; most commonly injured abdominal organ in blunt trauma; grades I-V (AAST)

FAST Exam.rationale: Bedside ultrasound; free fluid in Morison pouch and splenorenal recess

CT Abdomen with Contrast.rationale: If hemodynamically stable; grades injury and identifies active extravasation

Surgical Consult.rationale: Emergent splenectomy for hemodynamic instability; non-operative management for stable grade I-III

Angioembolization.rationale: For active blush on CT in hemodynamically stable patients

Blunt Abdominal Trauma - Liver Laceratio.pe_findings: RUQ pain, guarding, distension, peritoneal signs with grade IV-V; second most common organ injured in blunt trauma; hepatic veins/IVC junction injuries most lethal

FAST Exam.rationale: Free fluid in RUQ (Morison pouch/hepatorenal recess)

Massive Transfusion Protocol.rationale: 1:1:1 ratio; permissive hypotension (SBP 80-90) until surgical control

CT Abdomen.rationale: If stable; grade injury; active extravasation on arterial phase

Surgical Consult.rationale: Damage control laparotomy for hemodynamic instability; perihepatic packing

Angioembolization.rationale: For arterial blush in stable patients

Pneumothorax - Tension.pe_findings: Severe dyspnea, tracheal deviation AWAY from affected side, absent breath sounds, hyperresonance, distended neck veins, hypotension; obstructive shock; one-way valve mechanism

Needle Decompression.rationale: 14g needle, 2nd ICS midclavicular line or 5th ICS anterior axillary line; DO NOT WAIT for imaging — clinical diagnosis

Chest Tube.rationale: 28-36Fr tube thoracostomy after needle decompression; definitive treatment

CXR.rationale: Only AFTER decompression to confirm resolution; never delay treatment for imaging

Hemothorax - Massive.pe_findings: >1500mL blood in pleural space; decreased breath sounds, dullness to percussion, tracheal deviation (late), hemorrhagic shock; penetrating or blunt thoracic trauma

Chest Tube.rationale: 36-40Fr tube thoracostomy; allows drainage and autotransfusion

Thoracotomy Indications.rationale: >1500mL initial output OR >200mL/hr for 2-4h → emergent thoracotomy

Autotransfusion.rationale: Collect chest tube output for autotransfusion if available

Pelvic Fracture - Unstable with Hemorrha.pe_findings: Hemodynamic instability, pelvic instability on compression/distraction, perineal ecchymosis, scrotal/labial hematoma, blood at urethral meatus (urethral injury); high-energy mechanism (MVC, fall from height)

Pelvic Binder.rationale: Apply circumferential pelvic binder at level of greater trochanters; reduces pelvic volume and tamponades venous bleeding

FAST Exam.rationale: If positive → laparotomy first; if negative → angioembolization or preperitoneal packing

Angioembolization.rationale: For arterial bleeding identified on CT or angiography

Avoid Foley Until Cleared.rationale: If blood at urethral meatus → retrograde urethrogram before Foley

Traumatic Brain Injury - Severe.pe_findings: GCS ≤8, pupil asymmetry, posturing, Cushing response (hypertension/bradycardia/irregular respirations), scalp laceration/hematoma, Battle sign, raccoon eyes, CSF otorrhea/rhinorrhea

Intubation.rationale: GCS ≤8 = definitive airway; RSI avoiding ketamine myth (actually safe); avoid hypotension during RSI

CT Head STAT.rationale: Non-contrast; identify surgical lesion (EDH, SDH, ICH)

ICP Management.rationale: HOB 30°, mannitol 1g/kg or 23.4% NaCl if herniation signs; target ICP <22, CPP >60

Avoid Secondary Brain Injury.rationale: Target: SBP >100 (age 50-69) or >110 (15-49 or >70); PaO2 >60; normoglycemia; normothermia

Neurosurgery Consult.rationale: Emergent craniotomy for EDH, SDH with midline shift, or depressed skull fracture

Burns - Major (>20% TBSA).pe_findings: Full-thickness (painless, leathery, white/brown) or partial-thickness (painful, blistered, weeping); rule of 9s for TBSA; circumferential burns risk compartment syndrome; inhalation injury worsens prognosis

Parkland Formula.rationale: 4mL/kg/%TBSA LR over 24h; half in first 8h from TIME OF BURN; titrate to UOP 0.5-1mL/kg/hr

Airway Assessment.rationale: Intubate early if: facial burns, singed nasal hairs, soot in oropharynx, stridor, hoarseness

Escharotomy.rationale: For circumferential full-thickness burns with vascular compromise or chest restriction

Wound Care.rationale: Silver sulfadiazine or mafenide acetate; debridement

Burn Center Transfer.rationale: Major burns per ABA criteria → transfer to verified burn center

Compartment Syndrome - Acute.pe_findings: 5 P's: Pain (out of proportion, worse with passive stretch), Pressure (tense compartment), Paresthesias (early nerve ischemia), Paralysis (LATE), Pulselessness (VERY LATE - don't wait for this); tibial fracture most common
