Compartment Pressure Measurement.rationale: Stryker needle; absolute >30mmHg or delta pressure (diastolic - compartment) <30mmHg → fasciotomy

Emergent Fasciotomy.rationale: 4-compartment release for leg; ALL compartments must be opened; delay >6h → muscle necrosis → amputation

IV Fluid Resuscitation.rationale: Anticipate rhabdomyolysis after fasciotomy; aggressive NS for renal protection

Ectopic Pregnancy - Ruptured.pe_findings: Sudden severe lower abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding, signs of peritoneal irritation, shoulder pain (hemoperitoneum → diaphragmatic irritation), hemodynamic instability; positive pregnancy test

Surgical Consult (OB/GYN).rationale: Emergent laparoscopy/laparotomy for salpingectomy or salpingostomy

Quantitative hCG.rationale: Establishes pregnancy; level guides management in stable patients

FAST/Pelvic Ultrasound.rationale: Free fluid in cul-de-sac; empty uterus with adnexal mass/ring of fire

Placenta Previa - Active Bleeding.pe_findings: PAINLESS bright red vaginal bleeding (classic) in 2nd/3rd trimester; DO NOT perform digital vaginal exam; complete previa covers os; marginal/low-lying less severe

Transabdominal Ultrasound.rationale: CONFIRM previa; transvaginal is actually SAFE and more accurate; NO SPECULUM, NO DIGITAL EXAM until previa ruled out

Betamethasone.rationale: If <34 weeks and delivery not imminent; fetal lung maturity

Emergent C-Section.rationale: If uncontrollable hemorrhage or fetal distress; elective C-section at 36-37 weeks for stable complete previa

Blood Products Ready.rationale: Type and crossmatch 4 units pRBC; massive transfusion protocol on standby

HELLP Syndrome.pe_findings: Hemolysis + Elevated Liver enzymes + Low Platelets; RUQ/epigastric pain, nausea, malaise; may have minimal hypertension/proteinuria (atypical preeclampsia); hepatic rupture is life-threatening complication

Emergent Delivery.rationale: Definitive treatment; at any gestational age if maternal status is deteriorating

Magnesium Sulfate.rationale: Seizure prophylaxis; same dosing as eclampsia

CT Abdomen.rationale: If severe RUQ pain to evaluate for subcapsular hepatic hematoma/rupture

Amniotic Fluid Embolism.pe_findings: Sudden cardiovascular collapse during labor/delivery or immediate postpartum; triad: hypoxia, hypotension, DIC; 60-80% mortality; immunologic (anaphylactoid) reaction to fetal material in maternal circulation

Immediate Delivery.rationale: If undelivered; perimortem C-section within 5 minutes of cardiac arrest

Massive Transfusion + Cryo.rationale: Treat DIC aggressively: cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen, platelets, FFP

ICU Care.rationale: Mechanical ventilation, ECMO if available for refractory shock

No Specific Treatment.rationale: Supportive only; no proven targeted therapy; A-OK (atropine, ondansetron, ketorolac) protocol anecdotal

Uterine Rupture.pe_findings: Sudden abdominal pain (may feel 'pop'), loss of contractions, regression of fetal station, fetal bradycardia, vaginal bleeding, shock; prior C-section scar (VBAC) is major risk factor

Emergent Laparotomy.rationale: STAT C-section with uterine repair or hysterectomy; minutes matter for fetal survival

Massive Transfusion Protocol.rationale: Activate immediately; hemorrhage can be massive and rapid

Continuous Fetal Monitoring.rationale: Prolonged fetal bradycardia is the hallmark sign

Neonatal Resuscitation Team.rationale: Call NICU/peds team to delivery for fetal resuscitation

Cord Prolapse.pe_findings: Umbilical cord palpable in vagina ahead of presenting part after ROM; fetal bradycardia from cord compression; risk: polyhydramnios, malpresentation (footling breech, transverse), unengaged head, premature

Manual Elevation of Presenting Part.rationale: Push presenting part UP off cord with gloved hand in vagina; HOLD continuously until delivery

Emergent C-Section.rationale: Fastest route to delivery; team should be in OR within minutes

Bladder Filling.rationale: 500-750mL NS via Foley to elevate fetal head off cord (temporizing); controversial but effective bridge

DO NOT Attempt to Replace Cord.rationale: Attempting to push cord back increases vasospasm and compression

Pediatric Febrile Seizure - Complex.pe_findings: Seizure >15min or focal or recurrent within 24h in child 6mo-5y with fever; rule out meningitis in <12mo; post-ictal focal deficit warrants imaging

Benzodiazepine.rationale: Midazolam 0.2mg/kg IM/IN or diazepam 0.5mg/kg PR if no IV; lorazepam 0.1mg/kg IV if access available

LP Consideration.rationale: Recommended <12mo; consider 12-18mo; if pretreated with antibiotics or persistent altered mental status

Source Evaluation.rationale: UA, CXR, blood culture for fever source; treat underlying infection

Croup (Laryngotracheobronchitis).pe_findings: Barking/seal-like cough, inspiratory stridor, hoarse voice, steeple sign on AP neck XR; 6mo-3y peak; parainfluenza most common; Westley score for severity; worse at night

Dexamethasone.rationale: 0.6mg/kg PO/IM single dose; reduces return visits and intubation; effective for ALL severity levels

Nebulized Racemic Epinephrine.rationale: 0.5mL of 2.25% in 3mL NS; for moderate-severe stridor at rest; observe 2-4h for rebound

Heliox.rationale: 70:30 helium:oxygen for severe cases while awaiting steroid effect; reduces turbulent flow

Keep Child Calm.rationale: Avoid agitation — increases airway obstruction; let child sit in parent's lap; avoid unnecessary procedures

Epiglottitis - Pediatric/Adult.pe_findings: Drooling, dysphagia, distress, tripod positioning, muffled 'hot potato' voice; thumb sign on lateral neck XR; H. influenzae (pre-vaccine) or now S. aureus/Strep; COMPLETE airway obstruction risk

Airway Management.rationale: DO NOT examine throat or agitate child; take DIRECTLY to OR for controlled intubation with ENT/anesthesia backup for surgical airway

IV Antibiotics.rationale: Ceftriaxone 50mg/kg + vancomycin after airway secured; do NOT delay airway for IV access

Keep Child Calm.rationale: Absolute priority; allow comfortable position; avoid labs/imaging until airway secured

Dexamethasone.rationale: 0.6mg/kg IV to reduce airway edema after intubation

Bronchiolitis - Severe.pe_findings: Wheezing, crackles, tachypnea, nasal flaring, subcostal/intercostal retractions, poor feeding, apnea in young infants (<2mo); RSV most common; peak 2-6mo; winter seasonality

Supplemental O2.rationale: High-flow nasal cannula (HFNC) preferred over standard NC; reduces intubation rates

Nasal Suctioning.rationale: Gentle bulb or catheter suction; obligate nasal breathers — clearance improves feeding and breathing

IV Fluids.rationale: If unable to feed; maintain hydration; avoid overhydration

CPAP/HFNC.rationale: For moderate-severe: HFNC 2L/kg/min; CPAP for impending respiratory failure

Avoid Unnecessary Treatments.rationale: NO routine albuterol, NO steroids (unless prior wheeze history), NO antibiotics unless bacterial superinfection

Pyloric Stenosis.pe_findings: Projectile non-bilious vomiting in 2-8 week old; hungry after vomiting; palpable 'olive' mass RUQ (best after vomiting); visible gastric peristaltic waves; firstborn males most common

Correct Electrolytes FIRST.rationale: Hypochloremic hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis must be corrected before surgery; NS + KCl after UOP confirmed

Ultrasound.rationale: Pyloric muscle thickness >4mm, channel length >16mm; 95% sensitive and specific

Surgical Consult.rationale: Ramstedt pyloromyotomy (laparoscopic); definitive cure; NOT an emergency until electrolytes corrected

Neonatal Sepsis - Early Onset.pe_findings: Temperature instability (hypothermia MORE common than fever in neonates), poor feeding, lethargy, apnea, tachypnea, grunting, mottled/pale skin, hypoglycemia; <72h of life; GBS and E. coli predominate

Ampicillin + Gentamicin.rationale: Empiric within 1h of suspicion; Ampicillin 50mg/kg + Gentamicin 4mg/kg; add cefotaxime if meningitis suspected

Blood Culture.rationale: Before antibiotics if possible; but do NOT delay antibiotics for cultures

Lumbar Puncture.rationale: If stable enough; CSF culture, cell count, protein, glucose; delay if hemodynamically unstable

Glucose Monitoring.rationale: D10W bolus 2mL/kg for hypoglycemia; maintain glucose >45mg/dL

Volume Resuscitation.rationale: 10-20mL/kg NS bolus for hypotension; epinephrine drip if refractory

Meningococcemia.pe_findings: Rapidly progressive petechial→purpuric rash (non-blanching), meningismus, altered mental status, DIC, purpura fulminans (hemorrhagic skin necrosis); N. meningitidis; can progress from well to dead in hours

IV Ceftriaxone.rationale: 100mg/kg IV IMMEDIATELY; do NOT delay for LP; most critical intervention

Aggressive Fluid Resuscitation.rationale: 60mL/kg NS in first hour; may need 100-200mL/kg in first hours

DIC Management.rationale: Cryo for fibrinogen <100, platelets for <20K or active bleeding, FFP for INR >1.5

Chemoprophylaxis for Contacts.rationale: Rifampin, cipro, or ceftriaxone for close contacts; notify public health

Non-Accidental Trauma (Child Abuse).pe_findings: Injuries inconsistent with developmental stage or history; multiple bruises in various stages of healing; patterned injuries (belt, iron, cigarette); posterior rib fractures, metaphyseal corner fractures, retinal hemorrhages (shaken baby); delay in seeking care

Skeletal Survey.rationale: All children <2y; AP of all long bones, hands, feet, AP/lateral skull, AP/lateral spine, AP chest/pelvis

CT Head.rationale: If <1y, altered mental status, or suspicion of abusive head trauma; subdural hematoma classic

Social Work/CPS Report.rationale: MANDATORY reporting; failure to report is medical and legal error

Ophthalmology Consult.rationale: Dilated fundoscopic exam for retinal hemorrhages if head injury suspected

Documentation.rationale: Precise description of all injuries with measurements, photos, and exact quotes from caregivers

Acetaminophen Overdose - Acute.pe_findings: Phase 1 (0-24h): nausea, vomiting, malaise, diaphoresis; Phase 2 (24-72h): clinical improvement but rising LFTs and RUQ pain; Phase 3 (72-96h): hepatic necrosis, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, renal failure; Phase 4: recovery or death

N-Acetylcysteine (NAC).rationale: MOST efficacious within 8h; IV: 150mg/kg over 1h, then 50mg/kg over 4h, then 100mg/kg over 16h; continue if INR rising or liver failure

Serum APAP Level at 4h.rationale: Plot on Rumack-Matthew nomogram; treat if ABOVE treatment line

Activated Charcoal.rationale: 1g/kg PO if within 1-2h of ingestion and alert/protecting airway

King's College Criteria.rationale: Transplant criteria: pH <7.3 OR INR >6.5 + Cr >3.4 + grade 3-4 encephalopathy → transplant evaluation

Poison Center.rationale: 1-800-222-1222; guidance on NAC protocol and disposition

Salicylate (Aspirin) Toxicity.pe_findings: Tinnitus, nausea, vomiting, diaphoresis, tachypnea (respiratory alkalosis → mixed metabolic acidosis); altered mental status (late); ARDS, cerebral edema; primary respiratory alkalosis with anion gap metabolic acidosis is CLASSIC

Sodium Bicarbonate.rationale: 150mEq NaHCO3 in 1L D5W at 1.5-2x maintenance; target urine pH >7.5; alkalinize serum to trap salicylate in ionized form

D5W with Dextrose.rationale: CNS glucose may be low even with normal serum glucose; give D50 for altered mental status

Hemodialysis.rationale: Level >100mg/dL, renal failure, pulmonary edema, CNS symptoms, clinical deterioration despite treatment

Activated Charcoal.rationale: Multi-dose (q4h) for sustained-release preparations; aspirin forms bezoars

Avoid Intubation If Possible.rationale: Patient's tachypnea is compensatory; if must intubate, match the high minute ventilation to prevent acute acidemia → cardiac arrest

Opioid Overdose.pe_findings: Classic toxidrome: Respiratory depression (the killer), miosis (pinpoint pupils), CNS depression, decreased bowel sounds, hypothermia; needle tracks, nasal erosion; fentanyl: rapid onset, may need repeated naloxone

Naloxone (Narcan).rationale: 0.4-2mg IV/IM/IN; repeat q2-3min; titrate to respiratory drive (RR>12), NOT full consciousness; START LOW in known opioid-dependent to avoid acute withdrawal → pulmonary edema

Bag-Valve Mask.rationale: Ventilate BEFORE naloxone if severe; oxygenation is the priority

Naloxone Infusion.rationale: If repeated boluses needed (long-acting opioid): 2/3 of effective bolus dose per hour

Observe 4-6h minimum.rationale: Naloxone half-life (30-90min) shorter than most opioids; renarcotization risk; fentanyl patches may need 24h observation

Benzodiazepine Overdose.pe_findings: CNS depression, slurred speech, ataxia, hypotonia, respiratory depression (usually less severe than opioids unless co-ingestion); rarely fatal alone; co-ingestion with opioids/alcohol dramatically increases lethality

Supportive Care.rationale: Airway management, ventilatory support; usually sufficient alone

Flumazenil Contraindicated Usually.rationale: 0.2mg IV; AVOID in chronic benzo users (seizures), co-ingestion with proconvulsants (TCAs), or unknown ingestion; only for pure benzo OD in benzo-naive patient

Monitor for Co-Ingestion.rationale: Screen for opioids, alcohol, TCAs; co-ingestion is the real danger

Tricyclic Antidepressant (TCA) Overdose.pe_findings: Anticholinergic toxidrome (tachycardia, mydriasis, dry skin, urinary retention, ileus) PLUS sodium channel blockade (wide QRS >100ms, terminal R in aVR >3mm), seizures, hypotension; lethal in small amounts; amitriptyline most toxic

Sodium Bicarbonate.rationale: 1-2mEq/kg IV bolus for QRS >100ms; then infusion (150mEq in 1L D5W); target pH 7.50-7.55; overcomes sodium channel blockade

Continuous ECG Monitoring.rationale: QRS width predicts toxicity: >100ms = seizure risk, >160ms = arrhythmia risk

Avoid Contraindicated Drugs.rationale: NO class IA/IC antiarrhythmics (procainamide, flecainide); NO physostigmine; NO flumazenil (if co-ingested benzos)

Intralipid.rationale: 20% IV lipid emulsion for refractory cardiovascular collapse; 1.5mL/kg bolus then 0.25mL/kg/min

Seizure Management.rationale: Benzodiazepines ONLY (lorazepam 0.1mg/kg IV); NO phenytoin (worsens cardiac toxicity)

Sympathomimetic Toxidrome (Cocaine/Amphe.pe_findings: Hypertension, tachycardia, hyperthermia, agitation/psychosis, mydriasis, diaphoresis, seizures; cocaine-specific: chest pain (coronary vasospasm/MI), aortic dissection; amphetamine-specific: serotonin syndrome overlap possible

Benzodiazepines.rationale: FIRST-LINE for agitation, seizures, hypertension, tachycardia, chest pain; addresses sympathetic excess; diazepam 5-10mg IV or midazolam 5mg IM

Avoid Beta-Blockers.rationale: ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED — unopposed alpha stimulation → paradoxical hypertension; labetalol also avoided (weak beta >> alpha block)

Chest Pain Workup.rationale: ECG, troponin; nitro and benzodiazepines for coronary vasospasm; PCI if STEMI

Active Cooling.rationale: For hyperthermia >104°F; ice packs, evaporative cooling; rhabdomyolysis risk

Phentolamine.rationale: Alpha-blocker for refractory hypertension if benzos fail

Anticholinergic Toxidrome.pe_findings: 'Hot as a hare (hyperthermia), dry as a bone (anhydrosis), red as a beet (vasodilation), blind as a bat (mydriasis), mad as a hatter (delirium), full as a flask (urinary retention)'; absent bowel sounds, seizures; diphenhydramine, jimsonweed, TCAs

Physostigmine.rationale: 0.5-2mg IV slow push; for PURE anticholinergic toxicity with severe agitation/delirium; reversible AChE inhibitor; AVOID if TCA, QRS >100, or cardiac history

Benzodiazepines.rationale: For agitation/seizures if physostigmine contraindicated or unavailable

Active Cooling.rationale: Cannot sweat in anticholinergic toxicity; external cooling for hyperthermia

Foley Catheter.rationale: For urinary retention; must monitor volume

Activated Charcoal.rationale: If within 1-2h; note: decreased GI motility may extend absorption window

Organophosphate/Nerve Agent Poisoning.pe_findings: DUMBELS: Diarrhea, Urination, Miosis, Bradycardia/Bronchospasm/Bronchorrhea, Emesis, Lacrimation, Salivation; cholinergic crisis; muscle fasciculations, weakness, seizures; pesticides, sarin, VX

Atropine.rationale: 2mg IV q5min, doubling dose until (dry secretions); may need very large doses (100+ mg); does NOT reverse nicotinic effects (weakness/fasciculations)

Pralidoxime (2-PAM).rationale: 1-2g IV over 15-30min then infusion 500mg/hr; reactivates AChE before 'aging'; most effective early; reverses nicotinic effects

Decontamination.rationale: Remove all clothing, wash skin with soap and water; protect yourself FIRST — PPE for all providers

Benzodiazepines.rationale: Diazepam 10mg IV for seizures; midazolam IM if no IV

Intubation.rationale: For respiratory failure from bronchorrhea/bronchospasm/muscle weakness; avoid succinylcholine (prolonged paralysis)

Sickle Cell Vaso-Occlusive Crisis.pe_findings: Severe bone/joint pain, chest pain (acute chest syndrome), priapism, stroke, splenic sequestration; triggered by dehydration, cold, infection, hypoxia, stress; dactylitis in children

IV Opioid Analgesia.rationale: Morphine 0.1mg/kg or hydromorphone 0.015mg/kg IV q15min until controlled; do NOT undertreat pain; PCA when stabilized

IV Fluids.rationale: NS at 1-1.5x maintenance; avoid overhydration (ACS risk)

Acute Chest Syndrome Screening.rationale: CXR if fever >38.5°C, respiratory symptoms, or chest pain; new infiltrate + fever/respiratory symptoms = ACS

Exchange Transfusion.rationale: For HgbS >30% crisis, ACS, stroke, or priapism >4h; target HgbS <30%

Incentive Spirometry.rationale: q2h while awake; PROVEN to reduce ACS risk during vaso-occlusive crisis

Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (.pe_findings: Simultaneous thrombosis and hemorrhage; petechiae, purpura, oozing from lines/wounds, end-organ damage (renal, hepatic, CNS); triggers: sepsis, trauma, AML (especially APL), obstetric complications, pancreatitis

Treat Underlying Cause.rationale: MOST IMPORTANT; DIC is ALWAYS secondary; antibiotics for sepsis, delivery for obstetric cause, chemo for APL

Cryoprecipitate.rationale: 10 units for fibrinogen <100-150; each unit raises fibrinogen ~5-10mg/dL

Platelet Transfusion.rationale: Target >50K if bleeding; >20K prophylactic; may need ongoing due to consumption

Tranexamic Acid.rationale: Controversial; may help with hemorrhagic phenotype; avoid in acute thrombotic DIC; consider in obstetric DIC

Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia (HIT).pe_findings: Platelet drop >50% from baseline 5-10 days after heparin exposure (or sooner if prior exposure); PARADOXICALLY pro-THROMBOTIC (not bleeding); DVT, PE, limb ischemia, stroke; 4T score for probability

Stop ALL Heparin.rationale: Includes flushes, heparin-coated catheters, and LMWH; cross-reactivity with LMWH

Start Alternative Anticoagulant.rationale: Argatroban (direct thrombin inhibitor) preferred; bivalirudin for PCI; fondaparinux off-label; target PTT 1.5-3x baseline

4T Score.rationale: Calculate: Timing, Thrombocytopenia degree, Thrombosis, oTher causes; ≥4 = intermediate/high → send HIT antibody and treat while waiting

Duplex Ultrasound.rationale: Screen for DVT even if asymptomatic; 50% have subclinical thrombosis at diagnosis

Transition to Warfarin Carefully.rationale: Do NOT start warfarin until platelets >150K; overlap with argatroban 5+ days; premature warfarin → warfarin-induced skin necrosis/venous limb gangrene

Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura (TTP.pe_findings: Classic pentad (rarely all present): thrombocytopenia, MAHA (schistocytes), neurological symptoms (confusion, seizures, focal deficits), renal dysfunction, fever; ADAMTS13 deficiency; young women predominate

Emergent Plasma Exchange (PLEX).rationale: DEFINITIVE treatment; reduces mortality from 90% to <20%; daily until platelet normalization + LDH normal; start plasma infusion if PLEX delayed

Corticosteroids.rationale: Methylprednisolone 1g IV daily x 3 days then prednisone 1mg/kg

DO NOT TRANSFUSE Platelets.rationale: CONTRAINDICATED unless life-threatening hemorrhage; 'fuel on the fire' — triggers more thrombosis

ADAMTS13 Activity.rationale: Send BEFORE plasma exchange (dilutes level); <10% confirmatory; >20% consider alternative diagnosis

Rituximab.rationale: For refractory or relapsing; anti-CD20 antibody addresses autoimmune etiology

Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (ITP) -.pe_findings: Isolated thrombocytopenia with petechiae, purpura, mucosal bleeding (wet purpura = higher hemorrhage risk), menorrhagia; normal WBC and Hgb; no schistocytes (distinguishes from TTP/HUS); diagnosis of exclusion

Platelet Transfusion.rationale: Only for life-threatening bleeding or prior to emergent surgery; will be consumed rapidly but may be temporizing

IV Methylprednisolone.rationale: 1g IV daily x 3 days for severe; or dexamethasone 40mg IV daily x 4 days

IVIG.rationale: 1g/kg IV; for severe bleeding or pre-procedure; faster onset than steroids (24-48h); expensive

Tranexamic Acid.rationale: Adjunct for mucosal bleeding; holding pressure

Anti-D Immunoglobulin.rationale: 50mcg/kg IV for Rh-positive patients with intact spleen; avoid if hemolysis/anemia

Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS).pe_findings: FEVER + lead-pipe RIGIDITY + altered mental status + autonomic instability; develops over DAYS after starting/increasing antipsychotic or stopping dopaminergic; bradyreflexia (vs SS hyperreflexia); CK massively elevated

Stop Offending Agent.rationale: Discontinue ALL antipsychotics and anti-emetics (metoclopramide, prochlorperazine)

Dantrolene.rationale: 1-2.5mg/kg IV q5min; skeletal muscle relaxant; reduces rigidity and thermogenesis; continue 1mg/kg q4-8h

Bromocriptine.rationale: 2.5-5mg PO/NG q8h; dopamine agonist; restores dopaminergic tone; DO NOT give with dantrolene (some controversy)

Aggressive IV Fluids.rationale: Rhabdomyolysis prevention; NS at 200-300mL/hr initially; target UOP 200-300mL/hr

Active Cooling.rationale: External cooling for hyperthermia; benzodiazepines adjunct for rigidity

Acute Psychosis - Agitated/Violent.pe_findings: Acute behavioral emergency; disorganized speech, paranoia, hallucinations, agitation, self-harm risk; MUST rule out organic causes: hypoglycemia, hypoxia, head injury, intoxication, infection, thyroid, seizure

Verbal De-escalation.rationale: FIRST-LINE always; L.E.A.P technique; offer food/water/blanket; reduce stimuli; nonthreatening posture

Haloperidol + Lorazepam + Diphenhydramin.rationale: 'B52': haloperidol 5mg + lorazepam 2mg + diphenhydramine 50mg IM; or olanzapine 10mg IM (do NOT combine with IM benzos → respiratory depression)

Medical Clearance.rationale: Glucose, vitals, UDS, +/- CT head, +/- LP; rule out organic psychosis before psychiatric disposition

Physical Restraints.rationale: Last resort; 1:1 monitoring; reassess q15min; remove ASAP; document indication and monitoring

Suicidal Ideation with Plan - High Risk.pe_findings: Active suicidal ideation with specific plan, intent, and access to means; risk factors: prior attempt, psychiatric illness, substance use, recent loss, social isolation, male >65, chronic pain; assess lethal means

1:1 Observation.rationale: Constant visual observation; remove all potential means of self-harm from environment (sharps, cords, medications, belts)

Psychiatric Evaluation.rationale: Formal risk assessment including Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale (C-SSRS); determine inpatient vs outpatient level of care

Means Restriction.rationale: Lethal means counseling; remove access to firearms (most lethal method), medications, bridges

Medical Workup.rationale: Check APAP/salicylate levels, UDS, EtOH (co-ingestions); TSH; medical clearance

Safety Planning.rationale: If discharge planned: identify warning signs, coping strategies, contacts, restrict means; Crisis Text Line (741741), 988 Suicide Lifeline

Heat Stroke - Exertional.pe_findings: Core temp >104°F (40°C) + CNS dysfunction (confusion, seizures, coma); may or may not be sweating; exertional: young athletes/military; classic: elderly, medications, heat wave; rhabdomyolysis, DIC, liver/renal failure

Rapid Cooling.rationale: Target: reduce core temp to 101.3°F (38.5°C) within 30min; COLD WATER IMMERSION is best (reduces temp fastest); evaporative cooling if immersion unavailable

IV Fluids.rationale: NS 1-2L bolus; anticipate massive fluid requirements; monitor UOP

Avoid Antipyretics.rationale: NSAIDs and acetaminophen are INEFFECTIVE; heatstroke is NOT from prostaglandin elevation

Continuous Core Temperature.rationale: Rectal or esophageal probe; tympanic and axillary are inaccurate at extremes

Monitor for Complications.rationale: Rhabdomyolysis (CK q6h), DIC (coag panel), hepatic failure (LFTs may rise for 48-72h), renal failure

Hypothermia - Severe (<82.4\u00b0F / <28.pe_findings: Altered mental status, bradycardia, J/Osborn waves on ECG, VF risk below 82.4°F (28°C); 'not dead until warm and dead'; loss of shivering below 86°F; paradoxical undressing; atrial fibrillation common

Passive + Active External Rewarming.rationale: Remove wet clothes; warm blankets, Bair hugger; warm IV fluids (40-42°C); avoid rough handling (triggers VF)

Active Core Rewarming.rationale: For severe: warm humidified O2, warm peritoneal/pleural lavage, intravascular warming catheter; ECMO for cardiac arrest or refractory

Cardiac Monitoring.rationale: J waves, prolonged intervals, AF; one defibrillation attempt if VF; if unsuccessful, defer additional shocks until >30°C; continue CPR

Avoid Vasopressors Until Rewarmed.rationale: Drugs accumulate and may cause toxicity when patient warms and metabolism resumes; may give once if >30°C

Check Potassium.rationale: K >12 mEq/L associated with non-survivable injury; K <12 → aggressive rewarming

Drowning (Submersion Injury).pe_findings: Pulmonary edema (both fresh and saltwater), aspiration pneumonia/pneumonitis, ARDS, hypothermia; fresh water absorbed into pulmonary vasculature; saltwater pulls fluid into alveoli; 'dry drowning' (laryngospasm without aspiration) is largely a myth

Ventilatory Support.rationale: High-flow O2, CPAP, or intubation with PEEP (critical for surfactant washout); lung-protective ventilation for ARDS

C-Spine Precautions.rationale: If diving/trauma mechanism; maintain immobilization until cleared

Rewarming.rationale: If hypothermic; see hypothermia protocol; may require prolonged resuscitation ('not dead until warm and dead')

Observe 6-8h Minimum.rationale: All submersion victims; delayed pulmonary edema may develop; if asymptomatic with normal CXR at 6h → consider discharge

Bronchoscopy.rationale: If aspiration of particulate matter (sand, vomitus)

High Altitude Cerebral Edema (HACE).pe_findings: Ataxia + altered mental status at altitude; progression of AMS (acute mountain sickness); truncal ataxia on tandem gait is early sign; papilledema, retinal hemorrhages; above 2500m; vasogenic edema

Immediate Descent.rationale: DESCEND at least 300-1000m; single most important intervention; helicopter evacuation if unable to walk

Dexamethasone.rationale: 8mg IV/IM loading then 4mg q6h; reduces vasogenic edema; bridge until descent possible

Supplemental O2.rationale: Target SpO2 >90%; Gamow bag if supplemental O2 unavailable (portable hyperbaric chamber)

HAPE Coexistence.rationale: 50% of HACE patients have concurrent HAPE; nifedipine 30mg ER + O2 if pulmonary edema present

Anaphylaxis - Severe.pe_findings: Rapid onset (minutes to hours) of skin involvement (urticaria/angioedema) + respiratory compromise (stridor/wheeze/dyspnea) + hypotension/tachycardia; biphasic reaction in 5-20% (recurrence 1-72h); common triggers: food (nuts, shellfish), drugs (PCN, NSAIDs), Hymenoptera stings, latex

Epinephrine IM.rationale: 0.3-0.5mg (0.01mg/kg in children) IM mid-outer thigh; REPEAT q5-15min if needed; FIRST-LINE — no contraindications in anaphylaxis; delays increase mortality

IV Fluids.rationale: NS 1-2L rapid bolus for hypotension; may need 5-10L due to distributive shock and third spacing

Albuterol Nebulizer.rationale: 2.5-5mg for bronchospasm not responsive to epinephrine

Epinephrine Drip.rationale: For refractory hypotension: 1-4mcg/min; after 2-3 IM doses without response

Observation 4-6h minimum.rationale: Biphasic reaction risk; 12-24h observation for severe reactions; discharge with EpiPen prescription and allergy referral

Angioedema - Hereditary (HAE).pe_findings: Non-pruritic, non-pitting swelling of face/lips/tongue/larynx/intestine; NO urticaria (distinguishes from allergic angioedema); does NOT respond to epinephrine, antihistamines, or steroids; C1-INH deficiency; family history in 75%

C1-INH Concentrate.rationale: Berinert 20 units/kg IV; or Cinryze 1000 units IV; FIRST-LINE treatment; replaces deficient protein

Icatibant.rationale: Bradykinin B2 receptor antagonist; 30mg SC; alternative to C1-INH concentrate

Ecallantide.rationale: Kallikrein inhibitor; 30mg SC; monitor 30min for anaphylaxis risk (healthcare setting only)

Airway Management.rationale: Early intubation if airway involvement; may need surgical airway if rapid progression

Epinephrine + Antihistamines + Steroids.rationale: Bradykinin-mediated, NOT histamine-mediated; these drugs are ineffective but should be given while considering diagnosis

Systemic Lupus Erythematosus - Flare.pe_findings: Multi-organ flare: malar 'butterfly' rash, arthritis, serositis (pleural/pericardial effusion), nephritis (edema, proteinuria), CNS lupus (seizures, psychosis), cytopenias, fatigue, fever; triggered by UV exposure, infection, medication changes

Pulse Methylprednisolone.rationale: 1g IV daily x 3 days for severe flare (nephritis, cerebritis, severe cytopenias)

Hydroxychloroquine.rationale: Maintain/resume; reduces flares by 50%; should NEVER be stopped

Mycophenolate/Cyclophosphamide.rationale: For lupus nephritis; MMF preferred for induction in most (ALMS trial); cyclophosphamide for severe proliferative nephritis

Evaluate for Infection.rationale: Immunosuppressed patients: always rule out infection before treating flare as they may mimic each other

Renal Biopsy.rationale: Active urine sediment with rising creatinine → biopsy to classify ISN/RPS class; guides treatment
